**Ukraine**[[a]](#cite_note-10) is a country in [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"). It is the [second-largest country in Europe](/wiki/List_of_European_countries_by_area "List of European countries by area") after [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), which [borders it](/wiki/Russia%E2%80%93Ukraine_border "Russia–Ukraine border") to the east and northeast.[[b]](#cite_note-11) Ukraine also borders [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") to the north; [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia") to the west; [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") and [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova")[[c]](#cite_note-12) to the southwest; and the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") and the [Sea of Azov](/wiki/Sea_of_Azov "Sea of Azov") to the south and southeast.[[d]](#cite_note-13) [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv") is the nation's capital and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Ukraine "List of cities in Ukraine"), followed by [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv"), [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa "Odesa"), and [Dnipro](/wiki/Dnipro "Dnipro"). The [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of Ukraine is [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language").

Humans have inhabited Ukraine since 32,000 BC. During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), it was the site of [early Slavic](/wiki/Early_Slavs "Early Slavs") expansion and later became a key centre of [East Slavic](/wiki/East_Slavs "East Slavs") culture under the state of [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'"), which emerged in the 9th century. Kievan Rus' became the largest and most powerful realm in Europe in the 10th and 11th centuries, but gradually disintegrated into rival regional powers before being destroyed by the [Mongols](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Kievan_Rus%27 "Mongol invasion of Kievan Rus'") in the 13th century. For the next 600 years the area was contested, divided, and ruled by a variety of external powers, including the [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania"), the [Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Poland "Kingdom of Poland"), the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"), the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), and the [Tsardom of Russia](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Russia "Tsardom of Russia").

The [Cossack Hetmanate](/wiki/Cossack_Hetmanate "Cossack Hetmanate") emerged in [central Ukraine](/wiki/Central_Ukraine "Central Ukraine") in the 17th century but was partitioned between Russia and Poland before being gradually absorbed by the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") in the 18th century. [Ukrainian nationalism](/wiki/Ukrainian_nationalism "Ukrainian nationalism") developed and, following the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") in 1917, the short-lived [Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "Ukrainian People's Republic") was formed. The [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") [consolidated control](/wiki/Ukrainian%E2%80%93Soviet_War "Ukrainian–Soviet War") over much of the former empire and established the [Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic"), which became a [constituent republic](/wiki/Republics_of_the_Soviet_Union "Republics of the Soviet Union") of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") in 1922. In the early 1930s, millions of Ukrainians died in the [Holodomor](/wiki/Holodomor "Holodomor"), a [human-made famine](/wiki/Causes_of_the_Holodomor "Causes of the Holodomor"). During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Ukraine was [occupied by Germany](/wiki/Reichskommissariat_Ukraine "Reichskommissariat Ukraine") and endured [major battles and atrocities](/wiki/World_War_II_in_Ukraine "World War II in Ukraine"), resulting in 7 million civilians killed, including [most Ukrainian Jews](/wiki/The_Holocaust_in_Ukraine "The Holocaust in Ukraine").

Ukraine gained independence in 1991 as the [Soviet Union dissolved](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"), declaring itself [neutral](/wiki/Neutral_country "Neutral country").[[10]](#cite_note-gska2.rada.gov.ua-14) A new [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ukraine "Constitution of Ukraine") was adopted in 1996 as the country transitioned to a free market liberal democracy amid [endemic corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Ukraine#Political_corruption "Corruption in Ukraine") and a legacy of state control.[[11]](#cite_note-15) The [Orange Revolution](/wiki/Orange_Revolution "Orange Revolution") of 2004–2005 ushered electoral and constitutional reforms. Resurgent political crises prompted a series of mass demonstrations in 2014 known as the [Euromaidan](/wiki/Euromaidan "Euromaidan"), leading to [a revolution](/wiki/Revolution_of_Dignity "Revolution of Dignity"), at the end of which Russia unilaterally [occupied](/wiki/Russian_occupation_of_Crimea "Russian occupation of Crimea") and [annexed](/wiki/Annexation_of_Crimea_by_the_Russian_Federation "Annexation of Crimea by the Russian Federation") Ukraine's [Crimean Peninsula](/wiki/Crimea "Crimea"), and [pro-Russian unrest](/wiki/2014_pro-Russian_unrest_in_Ukraine "2014 pro-Russian unrest in Ukraine") culminated in [a war in Donbas](/wiki/War_in_Donbas_(2014%E2%80%932022) "War in Donbas (2014–2022)") with Russia and Russian-backed separatists. The [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine") began the [current phase of the war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)").[[12]](#cite_note-:5-16)[[13]](#cite_note-:6-17)[[14]](#cite_note-European_Commission_Trade_Ukraine-18)

Ukraine is a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") and [its system of government](/wiki/Government_of_Ukraine "Government of Ukraine") is a [semi-presidential republic](/wiki/Semi-presidential_republic "Semi-presidential republic"). Ukraine has a [transition economy](/wiki/Transition_economy "Transition economy") and has [the lowest nominal GDP per capita in Europe](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_in_Europe_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of sovereign states in Europe by GDP (nominal) per capita") as of 2024. Due to [its extensive fertile land](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Ukraine "Agriculture in Ukraine"), the country is an important exporter of [grain](/wiki/Grain "Grain"),[[15]](#cite_note-:9-19) though grain production has [declined](/wiki/2022_Russian_theft_of_Ukrainian_grain "2022 Russian theft of Ukrainian grain") since 2022 due to the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)"), endangering global [food security](/wiki/Food_security "Food security").[[16]](#cite_note-:8-20)[[15]](#cite_note-:9-19) Ukraine is considered a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power") in global affairs. [Its military](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_Ukraine "Armed Forces of Ukraine") is the [sixth largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_military_and_paramilitary_personnel "List of countries by number of military and paramilitary personnel") with the [eighth largest defence budget](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures "List of countries by military expenditures"), and [operates](/wiki/Unmanned_Systems_Forces_(Ukraine) "Unmanned Systems Forces (Ukraine)") one of the world's largest and most diverse [drone fleets](/wiki/Unmanned_combat_aerial_vehicle "Unmanned combat aerial vehicle"). Ukraine is a founding member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") and a member of the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), the [World Trade Organisation](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), and the [OSCE](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe"). It has been in the process of [joining the European Union](/wiki/Accession_of_Ukraine_to_the_European_Union "Accession of Ukraine to the European Union") and applied to join [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") in 2022.[[17]](#cite_note-21)

## Name

Main article: [Name of Ukraine](/wiki/Name_of_Ukraine "Name of Ukraine")

"The Ukraine" redirects here. For the book, see [*The Ukraine* (book)](/wiki/The_Ukraine_(book) "The Ukraine (book)").

The name of Ukraine is frequently interpreted as coming from the [old Slavic](/wiki/Old_Slavic "Old Slavic") term for 'borderland' as is the word *[krajina](/wiki/Krajina "Krajina")*.[[18]](#cite_note-22)
Another interpretation is that the name of Ukraine means "region" or "country".

In the [English-speaking world](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world") during most of the 20th century, Ukraine (whether independent or not) was referred to as "the Ukraine".[[19]](#cite_note-merriam-webster-23) This is because in Russian, the word *ukraina* means 'borderland'[[20]](#cite_note-24) so the [definite article](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)") would be natural in the English language; this is similar to **Nederlanden**, which means 'low lands' and is rendered in English as "*the* [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands")".[[21]](#cite_note-25) However, since Ukraine's [declaration of independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_of_Ukraine "Declaration of Independence of Ukraine") in 1991, this usage has become politicised and is now rarer, and [style guides](/wiki/Style_guide "Style guide") advise against its use.[[22]](#cite_note-UKrW812991TU-26)[[23]](#cite_note-27) U.S. ambassador [William Taylor](/wiki/William_B._Taylor_Jr. "William B. Taylor Jr.") said that using "the Ukraine" implies disregard for Ukrainian sovereignty.[[24]](#cite_note-28) The official Ukrainian position is that "the Ukraine" is both grammatically and politically incorrect.[[25]](#cite_note-BBC_News_Magazine-29)

## History

Main article: [History of Ukraine](/wiki/History_of_Ukraine "History of Ukraine")

### Early history

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Indo-European_migrations.jpg/330px-Indo-European_migrations.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indo-European_migrations.jpg)

Early [Indo-European migrations](/wiki/Indo-European_migrations "Indo-European migrations") from the [Pontic steppes](/wiki/Pontic_steppes "Pontic steppes") of present-day Ukraine and Russia[[26]](#cite_note-30)

Evidence for the earliest securely dated hominin presence in Europe comes from 1.4 million-year-old stone tools from [Korolevo](/wiki/Korolevo "Korolevo"), in western Ukraine.[[27]](#cite_note-Garba2024-31) Settlement by [modern humans](/wiki/Modern_humans "Modern humans") in Ukraine and its vicinity dates back to 32,000 BC, with evidence of the [Gravettian culture](/wiki/Gravettian_culture "Gravettian culture") in the [Crimean Mountains](/wiki/Crimean_Mountains "Crimean Mountains").[[28]](#cite_note-orig-32)[[29]](#cite_note-bbc-33) By 4,500 BC, the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") [Cucuteni–Trypillia culture](/wiki/Cucuteni%E2%80%93Trypillia_culture "Cucuteni–Trypillia culture") was flourishing in wide areas of modern Ukraine, including [Trypillia](/wiki/Trypillia "Trypillia") and the entire [Dnieper](/wiki/Dnieper "Dnieper")-[Dniester](/wiki/Dniester "Dniester") region. Ukraine is a probable location for the first [domestication of the horse](/wiki/Domestication_of_the_horse "Domestication of the horse").[[30]](#cite_note-34) The [Kurgan hypothesis](/wiki/Kurgan_hypothesis "Kurgan hypothesis") places the Volga-Dnieper region of Ukraine and southern Russia as the [linguistic homeland](/wiki/Linguistic_homeland "Linguistic homeland") of the [Proto-Indo-Europeans](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Europeans "Proto-Indo-Europeans").[[31]](#cite_note-35) Early [Indo-European migrations](/wiki/Indo-European_migrations "Indo-European migrations") from the Pontic steppes in the 3rd millennium BC spread [Yamnaya](/wiki/Yamnaya_culture "Yamnaya culture") [Steppe pastoralist](/wiki/Western_Steppe_Herders "Western Steppe Herders") ancestry and [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") across large parts of Europe.[[32]](#cite_note-36) During the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age"), the land was inhabited by [Iranian](/wiki/Eastern_Iranian_languages "Eastern Iranian languages")-speaking [Cimmerians](/wiki/Cimmerians "Cimmerians"), [Scythians](/wiki/Scythians "Scythians"), and [Sarmatians](/wiki/Sarmatians "Sarmatians").[[33]](#cite_note-37) Between 700 BC and 200 BC it was part of the [Scythian](/wiki/Scythia "Scythia") kingdom.[[34]](#cite_note-38)

From the 6th century BC, [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), and [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") colonies were established on the north-eastern shore of the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"), such as at [Tyras](/wiki/Tyras "Tyras"), [Olbia](/wiki/Olbia,_Ukraine "Olbia, Ukraine"), and [Chersonesus](/wiki/Chersonesus "Chersonesus"). These thrived into the 6th century AD. The [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths") stayed in the area, but came under the sway of the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns") from the 370s. In the 7th century, the territory that is now eastern Ukraine was the centre of [Old Great Bulgaria](/wiki/Old_Great_Bulgaria "Old Great Bulgaria"). At the end of the century, the majority of Bulgar tribes migrated in different directions, and the [Khazars](/wiki/Khazars "Khazars") took over much of the land.[[35]](#cite_note-39)

In the 5th and 6th centuries, the [Antes](/wiki/Antes_(people) "Antes (people)"), which some relate as an [early Slavic](/wiki/Early_Slavs "Early Slavs") people, lived in Ukraine. Migrations from the territories of present-day Ukraine throughout the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") established many [South Slavic](/wiki/South_Slavs "South Slavs") nations. Northern migrations, reaching almost to [Lake Ilmen](/wiki/Lake_Ilmen "Lake Ilmen"), led to the emergence of the [Ilmen Slavs](/wiki/Ilmen_Slavs "Ilmen Slavs") and [Krivichs](/wiki/Krivichs "Krivichs"). Following an [Avar](/wiki/Pannonian_Avars "Pannonian Avars") raid in 602 and the collapse of the Antes Union, most of these peoples survived as separate tribes until the beginning of the second millennium.[[36]](#cite_note-40)

### Golden Age of Kyiv

Main articles: [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'"), [Principality of Kiev](/wiki/Principality_of_Kiev "Principality of Kiev"), and [Kingdom of Galicia–Volhynia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia%E2%80%93Volhynia "Kingdom of Galicia–Volhynia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Principalities_of_Kievan_Rus%27_%281054-1132%29.jpg/330px-Principalities_of_Kievan_Rus%27_%281054-1132%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Principalities_of_Kievan_Rus%27_(1054-1132).jpg)

The furthest extent of [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'"), 1054–1132

The establishment of the state of [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'") remains obscure and uncertain.[[37]](#cite_note-41) The state included much of present-day Ukraine, Belarus and the western part of [European Russia](/wiki/European_Russia "European Russia").[[38]](#cite_note-Columbia-42) According to the *[Primary Chronicle](/wiki/Primary_Chronicle "Primary Chronicle")*, the [Rus' people](/wiki/Rus%27_people "Rus' people") initially consisted of [Varangians](/wiki/Varangian "Varangian") from [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia").[[39]](#cite_note-43) In 882, the pagan [Prince Oleg](/wiki/Oleg_of_Novgorod "Oleg of Novgorod") (Oleh) conquered [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv") from [Askold and Dir](/wiki/Askold_and_Dir "Askold and Dir") and proclaimed it as the new capital of the Rus'.[[40]](#cite_note-44) [Anti-Normanist](/wiki/Anti-Normanism "Anti-Normanism") historians however argue that the East Slavic tribes along the southern parts of the [Dnieper River](/wiki/Dnieper_River "Dnieper River") were already in the process of forming a state independently.[[41]](#cite_note-martin-45) The Varangian elite, including the ruling [Rurik dynasty](/wiki/Rurik_dynasty "Rurik dynasty"), later assimilated into the Slavic population.[[38]](#cite_note-Columbia-42) Kievan Rus' was composed of several [principalities](/wiki/Principality "Principality") ruled by the interrelated Rurikid *[kniazes](/wiki/Knyaz "Knyaz")* ("princes"), who often fought each other for possession of Kyiv.[[42]](#cite_note-46)

During the 10th and 11th centuries, Kievan Rus' became the largest and most powerful state in Europe, a period known as its Golden Age.[[43]](#cite_note-cia-47) It began with the reign of [Vladimir the Great](/wiki/Vladimir_the_Great "Vladimir the Great") (980–1015), who [introduced Christianity](/wiki/Christianization_of_Kievan_Rus%27 "Christianization of Kievan Rus'"). During the reign of his son, [Yaroslav the Wise](/wiki/Yaroslav_the_Wise "Yaroslav the Wise") (1019–1054), Kievan Rus' reached the zenith of its cultural development and military power.[[38]](#cite_note-Columbia-42) The state soon fragmented as the relative importance of regional powers rose again. After a final resurgence under the rule of [Vladimir II Monomakh](/wiki/Vladimir_II_Monomakh "Vladimir II Monomakh") (1113–1125) and his son [Mstislav](/wiki/Mstislav_I_of_Kiev "Mstislav I of Kiev") (1125–1132), Kievan Rus' finally disintegrated into separate principalities following Mstislav's death, though ownership of Kyiv would still carry great prestige for decades.[[44]](#cite_note-48) In the 11th and 12th centuries, the nomadic confederacy of the [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages")-speaking [Cumans](/wiki/Cumans "Cumans") and [Kipchaks](/wiki/Kipchaks "Kipchaks") was the dominant force in the [Pontic steppe](/wiki/Pontic_steppe "Pontic steppe") north of the Black Sea.[[45]](#cite_note-49)

The [Mongol invasions](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Rus%27 "Mongol invasion of Rus'") in the mid-13th century devastated Kievan Rus'; following the [Siege of Kyiv in 1240](/wiki/Siege_of_Kyiv_(1240) "Siege of Kyiv (1240)"), the city was destroyed by the Mongols.[[46]](#cite_note-50) In the western territories, the principalities of [Halych](/wiki/Principality_of_Halych "Principality of Halych") and [Volhynia](/wiki/Principality_of_Volhynia "Principality of Volhynia") had arisen earlier, and were merged to form the [Principality of Galicia–Volhynia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia%E2%80%93Volhynia "Kingdom of Galicia–Volhynia").[[47]](#cite_note-51) [Daniel of Galicia](/wiki/Daniel_of_Galicia "Daniel of Galicia"), son of [Roman the Great](/wiki/Roman_the_Great "Roman the Great"), re-united much of south-western Rus', including [Volhynia](/wiki/Volhynia "Volhynia"), [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Eastern_Europe) "Galicia (Eastern Europe)"), as well as Kyiv. He was subsequently crowned by a [papal](/wiki/Pope "Pope") envoy as the first [king of Galicia–Volhynia](/wiki/King_of_Ruthenia "King of Ruthenia") (also known as the Kingdom of [Ruthenia](/wiki/Ruthenia "Ruthenia")) in 1253.[[48]](#cite_note-52)

### Foreign domination

Further information: [Kiev Voivodeship](/wiki/Kiev_Voivodeship "Kiev Voivodeship")

See also: [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania"), [Crown of the Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland"), [Crimean Khanate](/wiki/Crimean_Khanate "Crimean Khanate"), [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), and [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Rzeczpospolita2nar.png/250px-Rzeczpospolita2nar.png)](/wiki/File:Rzeczpospolita2nar.png)

The [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") at its maximum extent in 1619, superimposed on modern borders. [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and the [Polish Crown](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland") exercised power over much of Ukraine after [1569](/wiki/Union_of_Lublin "Union of Lublin").   
   [Crown of the Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland")  
   [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania")  
   [Duchy of Livonia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Livonia "Duchy of Livonia")  
   [Duchy of Prussia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Prussia "Duchy of Prussia"), Polish [fief](/wiki/Fief "Fief")  
   [Duchy of Courland and Semigallia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Courland_and_Semigallia "Duchy of Courland and Semigallia"), Commonwealth fief

In 1349, in the aftermath of the [Galicia–Volhynia Wars](/wiki/Galicia%E2%80%93Volhynia_Wars "Galicia–Volhynia Wars"), the region was partitioned between the [Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Poland "Kingdom of Poland") and the [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania").[[49]](#cite_note-rowell266-53) From the mid-13th century to the late 1400s, the [Republic of Genoa](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa") founded numerous [colonies](/wiki/Colonies "Colonies") on the northern coast of the Black Sea and transformed these into large commercial centres headed by the consul, a representative of the Republic.[[50]](#cite_note-54) In 1430, the region of [Podolia](/wiki/Podolia "Podolia") was incorporated into Poland, and the lands of modern-day Ukraine became increasingly settled by [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people").[[51]](#cite_note-55) In 1441, [Genghisid](/wiki/Genghisid "Genghisid") prince [Haci I Giray](/wiki/Haci_I_Giray "Haci I Giray") founded the [Crimean Khanate](/wiki/Crimean_Khanate "Crimean Khanate") on the [Crimean Peninsula](/wiki/Crimean_Peninsula "Crimean Peninsula") and the surrounding steppes;[[52]](#cite_note-56) the Khanate orchestrated [Tatar](/wiki/Tatars "Tatars") [slave raids](/wiki/Crimean%E2%80%93Nogai_slave_raids_in_Eastern_Europe "Crimean–Nogai slave raids in Eastern Europe"). Over the next three centuries, the [Crimean slave trade](/wiki/Crimean_slave_trade "Crimean slave trade") would enslave an estimated two million in the region.[[53]](#cite_note-57)[[54]](#cite_note-58)

In 1569, the [Union of Lublin](/wiki/Union_of_Lublin "Union of Lublin") established the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), and most of the Ukrainian lands were transferred from Lithuania to the [Crown of the Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland"), becoming *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* Polish territory. Under the pressures of [Polonisation](/wiki/Polonisation "Polonisation"), many landed gentry of Ruthenia converted to [Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism") and joined the circles of the [Polish nobility](/wiki/Polish_nobility "Polish nobility"); others joined the newly created [Ruthenian Uniate Church](/wiki/Ruthenian_Uniate_Church "Ruthenian Uniate Church").[[55]](#cite_note-59)

### Cossack Hetmanate

Main articles: [Cossack Hetmanate](/wiki/Cossack_Hetmanate "Cossack Hetmanate") and [Zaporozhian Sich](/wiki/Zaporozhian_Sich "Zaporozhian Sich")

Deprived of native protectors among the Ruthenian nobility, the peasants and townspeople began turning for protection to the emerging [Zaporozhian Cossacks](/wiki/Zaporozhian_Cossacks "Zaporozhian Cossacks"). In the mid-17th century, a Cossack military quasi-state, the [Zaporozhian Host](/wiki/Zaporozhian_Host "Zaporozhian Host"), was formed by [Dnieper Cossacks](/wiki/Dnieper_Cossacks "Dnieper Cossacks") and Ruthenian peasants.[[56]](#cite_note-zaporizhia-60) Poland exercised little real control over this population, but found the Cossacks to be useful against the [Turks](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") and Tatars,[[57]](#cite_note-britcos-61) and at times the two were allies in [military campaigns](/wiki/Ottoman_wars_in_Europe "Ottoman wars in Europe").[[58]](#cite_note-62) However, the continued harsh [enserfment](/wiki/Serf "Serf") of Ruthenian peasantry by Polish [szlachta](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta") (many of whom were Polonised [Ruthenian nobles](/wiki/Ruthenian_nobility "Ruthenian nobility")) and the suppression of the Orthodox Church alienated the Cossacks.[[57]](#cite_note-britcos-61) The latter did not shy from taking up arms against those they perceived as enemies and occupiers, including the Catholic Church with its local representatives.[[59]](#cite_note-63)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Hondius_Bohdan_Khmelnytsky.jpg/250px-Hondius_Bohdan_Khmelnytsky.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hondius_Bohdan_Khmelnytsky.jpg)

[Hetman](/wiki/Hetmans_of_Ukrainian_Cossacks "Hetmans of Ukrainian Cossacks") [Bohdan Khmelnytsky](/wiki/Bohdan_Khmelnytsky "Bohdan Khmelnytsky") established an independent [Cossack state](/wiki/Cossack_Hetmanate "Cossack Hetmanate") after the [1648 uprising](/wiki/Khmelnytsky_Uprising "Khmelnytsky Uprising") against Poland

In 1648, [Bohdan Khmelnytsky](/wiki/Bohdan_Khmelnytsky "Bohdan Khmelnytsky") led the [largest of the Cossack uprisings](/wiki/Khmelnytsky_Uprising "Khmelnytsky Uprising") against the Commonwealth and the [Polish king](/wiki/List_of_Polish_monarchs "List of Polish monarchs"), which enjoyed wide support from the local population.[[60]](#cite_note-64) Khmelnytsky founded the [Cossack Hetmanate](/wiki/Cossack_Hetmanate "Cossack Hetmanate"), which existed until 1764 (some sources claim until 1782).[[61]](#cite_note-65) After Khmelnytsky suffered a crushing defeat at the [Battle of Berestechko](/wiki/Battle_of_Berestechko "Battle of Berestechko") in 1651, he turned to the [Russian tsar](/wiki/List_of_Russian_monarchs "List of Russian monarchs") for help. In 1654, Khmelnytsky was subject to the [Pereiaslav Agreement](/wiki/Pereiaslav_Agreement "Pereiaslav Agreement"), forming a military and political alliance with Russia that acknowledged loyalty to the Russian monarch.

After his death, the Hetmanate went through a devastating 30-year war amongst Russia, Poland, the Crimean Khanate, the Ottoman Empire, and [Cossacks](/wiki/Cossacks "Cossacks"), known as "[The Ruin](/wiki/The_Ruin_(Ukrainian_history) "The Ruin (Ukrainian history)")" (1657–1686), for control of the Cossack Hetmanate. The [Treaty of Perpetual Peace](/wiki/Treaty_of_Perpetual_Peace_(1686) "Treaty of Perpetual Peace (1686)") between Russia and Poland in 1686 divided the lands of the Cossack Hetmanate between them, reducing the portion over which Poland had claimed sovereignty to Ukraine west of the Dnieper river. In 1686, the [Metropolitanate of Kyiv](/wiki/Metropolitanate_of_Kyiv "Metropolitanate of Kyiv") was [annexed by the Moscow Patriarchate](/wiki/Annexation_of_the_Metropolitanate_of_Kyiv_by_the_Moscow_Patriarchate "Annexation of the Metropolitanate of Kyiv by the Moscow Patriarchate") through a synodal letter of the [Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarch_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople") [Dionysius IV](/wiki/Dionysius_IV_of_Constantinople "Dionysius IV of Constantinople"), thus placing the [Metropolitanate of Kyiv](/wiki/Metropolitanate_of_Kyiv#Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Metropolitanate of Kyiv") under the authority of [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"). An attempt to reverse the decline was undertaken by Cossack Hetman [Ivan Mazepa](/wiki/Ivan_Mazepa "Ivan Mazepa") (1639–1709), who ultimately defected to the [Swedes](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") in the [Great Northern War](/wiki/Great_Northern_War "Great Northern War") (1700–1721) in a bid to get rid of Russian dependence,[[62]](#cite_note-Magocsi-66) but Hetmanate's capital city [Baturyn](/wiki/Baturyn "Baturyn") was [sacked](/wiki/Sack_of_Baturyn "Sack of Baturyn") (1708) and they were crushed in the [Battle of Poltava](/wiki/Battle_of_Poltava "Battle of Poltava") (1709).[[63]](#cite_note-67)[[62]](#cite_note-Magocsi-66)

The Hetmanate's autonomy was severely restricted since Poltava. In the years 1764–1781, [Catherine the Great](/wiki/Catherine_the_Great "Catherine the Great") incorporated much of [Central Ukraine](/wiki/Central_Ukraine "Central Ukraine") into the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), abolishing the Cossack Hetmanate and the [Zaporozhian Sich](/wiki/Zaporozhian_Sich "Zaporozhian Sich"), and was one of the people responsible for the suppression of the last major Cossack uprising, the [Koliivshchyna](/wiki/Koliivshchyna "Koliivshchyna").[[64]](#cite_note-68) After the [annexation of Crimea by Russia](/wiki/Annexation_of_Crimea_by_the_Russian_Empire "Annexation of Crimea by the Russian Empire") in 1783, the newly acquired lands, now called [Novorossiya](/wiki/Novorossiya "Novorossiya"), were opened up to settlement by Russians.[[65]](#cite_note-69) The [tsarist autocracy](/wiki/Tsarist_autocracy "Tsarist autocracy") established a policy of [Russification](/wiki/Russification "Russification"), suppressing the use of the [Ukrainian language](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language") and curtailing the Ukrainian national identity.[[66]](#cite_note-censor-70) The western part of present-day Ukraine was subsequently split between Russia and [Habsburg](/wiki/Habsburg "Habsburg")-ruled [Austria](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") after the [fall](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland") of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth in 1795.

### 19th and early 20th century

Main articles: [Southwestern Krai](/wiki/Southwestern_Krai "Southwestern Krai"), [Kharkov Governorate](/wiki/Kharkov_Governorate "Kharkov Governorate"), [Chernigov Governorate](/wiki/Chernigov_Governorate "Chernigov Governorate"), [Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "Ukrainian People's Republic"), [Ukrainian State](/wiki/Ukrainian_State "Ukrainian State"), and [Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic")

Further information: [Ukrainian national revival](/wiki/Ukrainian_national_revival "Ukrainian national revival"), [Ukraine during World War I](/wiki/Ukraine_during_World_War_I "Ukraine during World War I"), [Ukraine after the Russian Revolution](/wiki/Ukraine_after_the_Russian_Revolution "Ukraine after the Russian Revolution"), [Ukrainian War of Independence](/wiki/Ukrainian_War_of_Independence "Ukrainian War of Independence"), and [Ukrainian–Soviet War](/wiki/Ukrainian%E2%80%93Soviet_War "Ukrainian–Soviet War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Polish_troops_in_Kiev.jpg/250px-Polish_troops_in_Kiev.jpg)](/wiki/File:Polish_troops_in_Kiev.jpg)

[Polish troops enter Kyiv](/wiki/Kiev_offensive_(1920) "Kiev offensive (1920)") in May 1920 during the [Polish–Soviet War](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Soviet_War "Polish–Soviet War"). Following the [Peace of Riga](/wiki/Peace_of_Riga "Peace of Riga") signed on 18 March 1921, Poland took control of modern-day western Ukraine while Soviets took control of eastern and central Ukraine

The 19th century saw the rise of Ukrainian nationalism. With growing urbanisation and modernisation and a cultural trend toward [romantic nationalism](/wiki/Romantic_nationalism "Romantic nationalism"), a Ukrainian [intelligentsia](/wiki/Intelligentsia "Intelligentsia") committed to national rebirth and social justice emerged. The serf-turned-national-poet [Taras Shevchenko](/wiki/Taras_Shevchenko "Taras Shevchenko") (1814–1861) and political theorist [Mykhailo Drahomanov](/wiki/Mykhailo_Drahomanov "Mykhailo Drahomanov") (1841–1895) led the growing nationalist movement.[[67]](#cite_note-71)[[68]](#cite_note-72) While conditions for its development in Austrian [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(eastern_Europe) "Galicia (eastern Europe)") under the [Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburgs "Habsburgs") were relatively lenient,[[69]](#cite_note-73) the Russian part (historically known as "[Little Russia](/wiki/Little_Russia "Little Russia")" or "South Russia")[[70]](#cite_note-74) faced severe restrictions, going as far as [banning virtually all books from being published in Ukrainian](/wiki/Ems_Ukaz "Ems Ukaz") in 1876.

Ukraine, like the rest of the Russian Empire, joined the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") [later](/wiki/Industrialization_in_the_Russian_Empire "Industrialization in the Russian Empire") than most of Western Europe[[71]](#cite_note-75)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] due to the maintenance of [serfdom](/wiki/Serfdom_in_Russia "Serfdom in Russia") until 1861.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Other than near the newly discovered coal fields of the [Donbas](/wiki/Donbas "Donbas"), and in some larger cities such as [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa "Odesa") and Kyiv, Ukraine largely remained an agricultural and resource extraction economy.[[72]](#cite_note-76) The Austrian part of Ukraine [was particularly destitute](/wiki/Poverty_in_Austrian_Galicia "Poverty in Austrian Galicia"), which forced hundreds of thousands of peasants into emigration, who created the backbone of an extensive [Ukrainian diaspora](/wiki/Ukrainian_diaspora "Ukrainian diaspora") in countries such as [Canada](/wiki/Ukrainian_Canadians "Ukrainian Canadians"), the [United States](/wiki/Ukrainian_Americans "Ukrainian Americans") and [Brazil](/wiki/Ukrainian_Brazilians "Ukrainian Brazilians").[[73]](#cite_note-77) Some of the Ukrainians settled in the Far East, too. According to the [1897 census](/wiki/1897_census "1897 census"), there were 223,000 ethnic Ukrainians in [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") and 102,000 in [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia").[[74]](#cite_note-78) An additional 1.6 million emigrated to the east in the ten years after the opening of the [Trans-Siberian Railway](/wiki/Trans-Siberian_Railway "Trans-Siberian Railway") in 1906.[[75]](#cite_note-79) [Far Eastern](/wiki/Russian_Far_East "Russian Far East") areas with an ethnic Ukrainian population became known as [Green Ukraine](/wiki/Green_Ukraine "Green Ukraine").[[76]](#cite_note-80)

Ukraine plunged into turmoil with the beginning of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), and fighting on Ukrainian soil persisted until late 1921. Initially, the Ukrainians were split between Austria-Hungary, fighting for the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers"), though the vast majority served in the [Imperial Russian Army](/wiki/Military_history_of_Imperial_Russia "Military history of Imperial Russia"), which was part of the [Triple Entente](/wiki/Triple_Entente "Triple Entente"), under Russia.[[77]](#cite_note-81) As the Russian Empire collapsed, the conflict evolved into the [Ukrainian War of Independence](/wiki/Ukrainian_War_of_Independence "Ukrainian War of Independence"), with Ukrainians fighting alongside, or against, the [Red](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army"), [White](/wiki/White_Army "White Army"), [Black](/wiki/Makhnovshchina "Makhnovshchina") and [Green armies](/wiki/Green_armies "Green armies"), with the Poles, Hungarians (in [Transcarpathia](/wiki/Transcarpathian_Rus%27 "Transcarpathian Rus'")), and Germans also intervening at various times.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Ukrainian_national_costumes_04.jpg/250px-Ukrainian_national_costumes_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ukrainian_national_costumes_04.jpg)

Youth in national Ukrainian dress during a ceremony commemorating the 22nd January 1919 "Act of Reunification of the Ukrainian People's Republic and the Western Ukrainian People's Republic", which is honoured yearly across 22 cities of Ukraine

An attempt to create an independent state, the left-leaning [Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "Ukrainian People's Republic") (UPR), was first announced by [Mykhailo Hrushevsky](/wiki/Mykhailo_Hrushevsky "Mykhailo Hrushevsky"), but the period was plagued by an extremely unstable political and military environment. It was first deposed in a [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") led by [Pavlo Skoropadskyi](/wiki/Pavlo_Skoropadskyi "Pavlo Skoropadskyi"), which yielded the [Ukrainian State](/wiki/Ukrainian_State "Ukrainian State") under the German protectorate, and the attempt to restore the UPR under the [Directorate](/wiki/Directorate_of_Ukraine "Directorate of Ukraine") ultimately failed as the Ukrainian army was regularly overrun by other forces. The short-lived [West Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/West_Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "West Ukrainian People's Republic") and [Hutsul Republic](/wiki/Hutsul_Republic "Hutsul Republic") also failed to join the rest of Ukraine.[[78]](#cite_note-82)

The result of the conflict was a partial victory for the [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic"), which annexed the Western Ukrainian provinces, as well as a larger-scale victory for the pro-Soviet forces, which succeeded in dislodging the remaining factions and eventually established the [Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic") (Soviet Ukraine). Meanwhile, modern-day [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina") was occupied by [Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania") and [Carpathian Ruthenia](/wiki/Carpathian_Ruthenia "Carpathian Ruthenia") was admitted to [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/First_Czechoslovak_Republic "First Czechoslovak Republic") as an autonomous region.[[79]](#cite_note-83)

The conflict over Ukraine, a part of the broader [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War"), devastated the whole of the former Russian Empire, including eastern and central Ukraine. The fighting left over 1.5 million people dead and hundreds of thousands homeless in the former Russian Empire's territory. [Famine in 1921](/wiki/Russian_famine_of_1921%E2%80%931922 "Russian famine of 1921–1922") further hit the eastern provinces.[[80]](#cite_note-84)[[81]](#cite_note-85)

### Inter-war period

See also: [Holodomor](/wiki/Holodomor "Holodomor")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/HolodomorKharkiv_1933_Wienerberger.jpg/250px-HolodomorKharkiv_1933_Wienerberger.jpg)](/wiki/File:HolodomorKharkiv_1933_Wienerberger.jpg)

Starved peasants on a street in [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv"), 1933. [Collectivisation](/wiki/Collectivisation "Collectivisation") of crops and their confiscation by Soviet authorities led to a major famine in Soviet Ukraine known as the [Holodomor](/wiki/Holodomor "Holodomor")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Les_Kurbas_Portrait.jpg/250px-Les_Kurbas_Portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Les_Kurbas_Portrait.jpg)

[Les Kurbas](/wiki/Les_Kurbas "Les Kurbas"), one of the lead figures of the [Executed Renaissance](/wiki/Executed_Renaissance "Executed Renaissance"), was executed by the Soviet authorities, as many other Ukrainian intellectuals[[82]](#cite_note-86)[[83]](#cite_note-Kravchenko-87)

During the inter-war period, in Poland, Marshal [Józef Piłsudski](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Pi%C5%82sudski "Józef Piłsudski") sought Ukrainian support by offering local autonomy as a way to minimise Soviet influence in Poland's eastern [Kresy](/wiki/Kresy "Kresy") region.[[84]](#cite_note-88)[[85]](#cite_note-89) However, this approach was abandoned after Piłsudski's death in 1935, due to continued unrest among the Ukrainian population, including assassinations of Polish government officials by the [Organisation of Ukrainian Nationalists](/wiki/Organisation_of_Ukrainian_Nationalists "Organisation of Ukrainian Nationalists") (OUN); with the Polish government responding by restricting rights of people who declared Ukrainian nationality.[[86]](#cite_note-auto2-90)[[87]](#cite_note-auto3-91) In consequence, the underground [Ukrainian nationalist](/wiki/Ukrainian_nationalism "Ukrainian nationalism") and militant movement, which arose in the 1920s gained wider support.

Meanwhile, the recently constituted Soviet Ukraine became one of the founding republics of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). During the 1920s,[[88]](#cite_note-92) under the Ukrainisation policy pursued by the national Communist leadership of [Mykola Skrypnyk](/wiki/Mykola_Skrypnyk "Mykola Skrypnyk"), Soviet leadership at first encouraged a national renaissance in [Ukrainian culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Ukraine "Culture of Ukraine") and language. [Ukrainisation](/wiki/Ukrainisation "Ukrainisation") was part of the Soviet-wide policy of [Korenisation](/wiki/Korenisation "Korenisation") (literally *indigenisation*), which was intended to promote the advancement of native peoples, their language and culture into the governance of their respective republics.

Around the same time, Soviet leader [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") instituted the [New Economic Policy](/wiki/New_Economic_Policy "New Economic Policy") (NEP), which introduced a form of [market socialism](/wiki/Market_socialism "Market socialism"), allowing some private ownership of small and medium-sized productive enterprises, hoping to reconstruct the post-war Soviet Union that had been devastated by both WWI and later the civil war. The NEP was successful at restoring the formerly war-torn nation to pre-WWI levels of production and agricultural output by the mid-1920s, much of the latter based in Ukraine.[[89]](#cite_note-Service-93) These policies attracted many prominent former UNR figures, including former UNR leader Hrushevsky, to return to Soviet Ukraine, where they were accepted, and participated in the advancement of Ukrainian science and culture.[[90]](#cite_note-94) In July 1922, arrests and [deportations](/wiki/Population_transfer_in_the_Soviet_Union "Population transfer in the Soviet Union") of Ukrainian intellectuals (e.g. university professors) began in Soviet Ukraine and continued throughout the 1920s.[[91]](#cite_note-95)

This period was cut short when [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") became the leader of the USSR following Lenin's death. Stalin did away with the NEP in what became known as the [Great Break](/wiki/Great_Break_(USSR) "Great Break (USSR)"). Starting from the late 1920s and now with a [centrally planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy"), Soviet Ukraine took part in an [industrialisation scheme](/wiki/Industrialization_in_the_USSR "Industrialization in the USSR") which quadrupled its industrial output during the 1930s.

Nevertheless, Stalin sought to prevent the Ukrainians aspirations for the independence of Ukraine and took severe measures to eliminate Ukrainian peasantry and elite Ukrainian intellectuals and culturists.[[92]](#cite_note-96)[[83]](#cite_note-Kravchenko-87) As a consequence of Stalin's new policy, the Ukrainian peasantry suffered from the [programme of collectivisation](/wiki/Collectivization_in_the_USSR "Collectivization in the USSR") of agricultural crops. Collectivisation was part of the [first five-year plan](/wiki/First_five-year_plan_(Soviet_Union) "First five-year plan (Soviet Union)") and was enforced by regular troops and the secret police known as [Cheka](/wiki/Cheka "Cheka"). Those who resisted were [arrested and deported](/wiki/Population_transfer_in_the_Soviet_Union "Population transfer in the Soviet Union") to [gulags](/wiki/Gulag "Gulag") and work camps. As members of the collective farms were sometimes not allowed to receive any grain until unrealistic quotas were met, millions starved to death in a [famine](/wiki/Droughts_and_famines_in_Russia_and_the_Soviet_Union "Droughts and famines in Russia and the Soviet Union") known as the [Holodomor](/wiki/Holodomor "Holodomor") or the "Great Famine", which was recognised by some countries as an act of [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide") perpetrated by Joseph Stalin and other Soviet notables.[[93]](#cite_note-97)

Following on the Russian Civil War and collectivisation, the [Great Purge](/wiki/Great_Purge "Great Purge"), while killing Stalin's perceived political enemies, resulted in a profound loss of a new generation of Ukrainian intelligentsia, known today as the [Executed Renaissance](/wiki/Executed_Renaissance "Executed Renaissance").[[94]](#cite_note-98)[[95]](#cite_note-99)[[96]](#cite_note-100)[[97]](#cite_note-101)

### World War II

See also: [Eastern Front (World War II)](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_II) "Eastern Front (World War II)"), [Reichskommissariat Ukraine](/wiki/Reichskommissariat_Ukraine "Reichskommissariat Ukraine"), and [The Holocaust in Ukraine](/wiki/The_Holocaust_in_Ukraine "The Holocaust in Ukraine")

Following the [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland") in September 1939, [German](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Soviet](/wiki/Union_of_Soviet_Socialist_Republics "Union of Soviet Socialist Republics") troops divided the territory of Poland. Thus, Eastern Galicia and Volhynia with their Ukrainian population became part of Ukraine. For the first time in history, the nation was united.[[98]](#cite_note-102)[[99]](#cite_note-103) Further territorial gains were secured in 1940, when the Ukrainian SSR incorporated the northern and southern districts of [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia"), [Northern Bukovina](/wiki/Northern_Bukovina "Northern Bukovina"), and the [Hertsa region](/wiki/Hertsa_region "Hertsa region") from the territories the USSR [forced Romania to cede](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Bessarabia_and_Northern_Bukovina "Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina"), though it handed over the western part of the [Moldavian Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Moldavian_Autonomous_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Moldavian Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic") to the newly created [Moldavian SSR](/wiki/Moldavian_SSR "Moldavian SSR"). These territorial gains of the USSR were internationally recognised by the [Paris peace treaties of 1947](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Treaties,_1947 "Paris Peace Treaties, 1947").[[100]](#cite_note-104)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/%D0%9C%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%88%D0%B0%D0%BB_%D0%A1%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%A1%D0%BE%D1%8E%D0%B7%D0%B0_%D0%93%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B9_%D0%A1%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%A1%D0%BE%D1%8E%D0%B7%D0%B0_%D0%A1%D0%B5%D0%BC%D1%91%D0%BD_%D0%9A%D0%BE%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%87_%D0%A2%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE.jpg/250px-%D0%9C%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%88%D0%B0%D0%BB_%D0%A1%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%A1%D0%BE%D1%8E%D0%B7%D0%B0_%D0%93%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B9_%D0%A1%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%A1%D0%BE%D1%8E%D0%B7%D0%B0_%D0%A1%D0%B5%D0%BC%D1%91%D0%BD_%D0%9A%D0%BE%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%87_%D0%A2%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9C%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%88%D0%B0%D0%BB_%D0%A1%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%A1%D0%BE%D1%8E%D0%B7%D0%B0_%D0%93%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B9_%D0%A1%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%A1%D0%BE%D1%8E%D0%B7%D0%B0_%D0%A1%D0%B5%D0%BC%D1%91%D0%BD_%D0%9A%D0%BE%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%87_%D0%A2%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE.jpg)

Marshal [Semyon Timoshenko](/wiki/Semyon_Timoshenko "Semyon Timoshenko") (born in the [Budjak](/wiki/Budjak "Budjak") region) commanded numerous fronts throughout the war, including the [Southwestern Front](/wiki/Southwestern_Front_(Soviet_Union) "Southwestern Front (Soviet Union)") east of Kyiv in 1941.

[German armies](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht") [invaded the Soviet Union](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa") on 22 June 1941, initiating nearly four years of [total war](/wiki/Total_war "Total war"). The [Axis](/wiki/Axis_Powers "Axis Powers") initially advanced against desperate but unsuccessful efforts of the Red Army. In the [battle of Kyiv](/wiki/Battle_of_Kyiv_(1941) "Battle of Kyiv (1941)"), the city was acclaimed as a "[Hero City](/wiki/Hero_City_(Soviet_Union) "Hero City (Soviet Union)")", because of its fierce resistance. More than 600,000 Soviet soldiers (or one-quarter of the [Soviet Western Front](/wiki/Soviet_Western_Front "Soviet Western Front")) were killed or taken captive there, with many suffering [severe mistreatment](/wiki/German_mistreatment_of_Soviet_prisoners_of_war "German mistreatment of Soviet prisoners of war").[[101]](#cite_note-105)[[102]](#cite_note-106) After its conquest, most of the Ukrainian SSR was organised within the [Reichskommissariat Ukraine](/wiki/Reichskommissariat_Ukraine "Reichskommissariat Ukraine"), with the intention of exploiting its resources and eventual German settlement. Some western Ukrainians, who had only joined the Soviet Union in 1939, hailed the Germans as liberators, but that did not last long as the Nazis made little attempt to exploit dissatisfaction with Stalinist policies.[[103]](#cite_note-ww2-107) Instead, the Nazis preserved the collective-farm system, carried out [genocidal policies](/wiki/Mass_graves_in_the_Soviet_Union "Mass graves in the Soviet Union") against [Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Ukraine "History of the Jews in Ukraine"), [deported millions of people to work in Germany](/wiki/OST-Arbeiter "OST-Arbeiter"), and began a depopulation programme to prepare for German colonisation.[[103]](#cite_note-ww2-107) They blockaded the transport of food on the Dnieper River.[[104]](#cite_note-108)

Although the majority of Ukrainians fought in or alongside the Red Army and [Soviet resistance](/wiki/Soviet_partisans "Soviet partisans"),[[105]](#cite_note-worldwars-109) in Western Ukraine an independent [Ukrainian Insurgent Army](/wiki/Ukrainian_Insurgent_Army "Ukrainian Insurgent Army") movement arose (UPA, 1942). It was created as the armed forces of the underground [Organisation of Ukrainian Nationalists](/wiki/Organization_of_Ukrainian_Nationalists "Organization of Ukrainian Nationalists") (OUN).[[106]](#cite_note-110)[[107]](#cite_note-vedeneyev-111) Both organisations, the OUN and the UPA, supported the goal of an [independent Ukrainian state](/wiki/Declaration_of_Ukrainian_Independence,_1941 "Declaration of Ukrainian Independence, 1941") on the territory with a Ukrainian ethnic majority. Although this brought conflict with Nazi Germany, at times the [Melnyk wing of the OUN](/wiki/Melnykites "Melnykites") allied with the Nazi forces. From mid-1943 until the end of the war, the UPA carried out [massacres of ethnic Poles](/wiki/Massacres_of_Poles_in_Volhynia_and_Eastern_Galicia "Massacres of Poles in Volhynia and Eastern Galicia") in the Volhynia and [Eastern Galicia](/wiki/Eastern_Galicia "Eastern Galicia") regions, killing around 100,000 Polish civilians, which brought reprisals.[[108]](#cite_note-112)[[109]](#cite_note-IPNconf-113) These organised massacres were an attempt by the OUN to create a homogeneous Ukrainian state without a Polish minority living within its borders, and to prevent the post-war Polish state from asserting its sovereignty over areas that had been part of pre-war Poland.[[110]](#cite_note-114) After the war, the UPA continued to fight the USSR until the 1950s.[[111]](#cite_note-115)[[112]](#cite_note-116) At the same time, the [Ukrainian Liberation Army](/wiki/Ukrainian_Liberation_Army "Ukrainian Liberation Army"), another nationalist movement, fought alongside the Nazis.[[113]](#cite_note-Kalb2015-117)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Ruined_Kiev_in_WWII.jpg/250px-Ruined_Kiev_in_WWII.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ruined_Kiev_in_WWII.jpg)

[Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv") suffered significant damage during [World War II](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_II) "Eastern Front (World War II)"), and was occupied by the [Germans](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht") from 19 September 1941 until 6 November 1943

In total, the number of ethnic Ukrainians who fought in the ranks of the Soviet Army is estimated from 4.5 million[[105]](#cite_note-worldwars-109) to 7 million;[[114]](#cite_note-118)[[e]](#cite_note-fn1-119) half of the [Pro-Soviet partisan](/wiki/Soviet_Partisans "Soviet Partisans") guerrilla resistance units, which counted up to 500,000 troops in 1944, were also Ukrainian.[[115]](#cite_note-120) Generally, the Ukrainian Insurgent Army's figures are unreliable, with figures ranging anywhere from 15,000 to as many as 100,000 fighters.[[116]](#cite_note-121)[[117]](#cite_note-122)

The vast majority of the fighting in World War II took place on the [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_II) "Eastern Front (World War II)").[[118]](#cite_note-123) The [total losses](/wiki/World_War_II_casualties_of_the_Soviet_Union "World War II casualties of the Soviet Union") inflicted upon the Ukrainian population during the war are estimated at 6 million,[[119]](#cite_note-124)[[120]](#cite_note-dt-kul-dem-los-125) including an estimated one and a half million Jews killed by the [Einsatzgruppen](/wiki/Einsatzgruppen "Einsatzgruppen"),[[121]](#cite_note-126) sometimes with the help of local collaborators. Of the estimated 8.6 million Soviet troop losses,[[122]](#cite_note-peremoga7-127)[[123]](#cite_note-128)[[124]](#cite_note-Krivosheev-129) 1.4 million were ethnic [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians").[[122]](#cite_note-peremoga7-127)[[124]](#cite_note-Krivosheev-129)[[e]](#cite_note-fn1-119)[[f]](#cite_note-130) The [Victory Day](/wiki/Victory_Day_over_Nazism_in_World_War_II "Victory Day over Nazism in World War II") is celebrated as one of eleven Ukrainian national holidays.[[125]](#cite_note-131)

### Post–war Soviet Ukraine

Further information: [Anti-Soviet resistance by the Ukrainian Insurgent Army](/wiki/Anti-Soviet_resistance_by_the_Ukrainian_Insurgent_Army "Anti-Soviet resistance by the Ukrainian Insurgent Army") and [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster")

See also: [History of the Soviet Union (1953–1964)](/wiki/History_of_the_Soviet_Union_(1953%E2%80%931964) "History of the Soviet Union (1953–1964)"), [History of the Soviet Union (1964–1982)](/wiki/History_of_the_Soviet_Union_(1964%E2%80%931982) "History of the Soviet Union (1964–1982)"), and [History of the Soviet Union (1982–1991)](/wiki/History_of_the_Soviet_Union_(1982%E2%80%931991) "History of the Soviet Union (1982–1991)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/c/c8/Khrushchev_and_Brezhnev.jpg/250px-Khrushchev_and_Brezhnev.jpg)](/wiki/File:Khrushchev_and_Brezhnev.jpg)

Two future leaders of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") (left, pre-war [CPSU](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union") chief in Ukraine) and [Leonid Brezhnev](/wiki/Leonid_Brezhnev "Leonid Brezhnev") (an engineer from [Kamianske](/wiki/Kamianske "Kamianske"), Ukraine)

The republic was heavily damaged by the war, and it required significant efforts to recover. More than 700 cities and towns and 28,000 villages were destroyed.[[126]](#cite_note-132) The situation was worsened by a [famine](/wiki/Famine "Famine") in 1946–1947, which was caused by a drought and the wartime destruction of infrastructure, killing at least tens of thousands of people.[[120]](#cite_note-dt-kul-dem-los-125) In 1945, the Ukrainian SSR became one of the founding members of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") (UN),[[127]](#cite_note-un_ukssr-133) part of a special agreement at the [Yalta Conference](/wiki/Yalta_Conference "Yalta Conference"), and, alongside Belarus, had voting rights in the UN even though they were not independent.[[128]](#cite_note-134)[[129]](#cite_note-135) Moreover, Ukraine once more expanded its borders as it annexed [Zakarpattia](/wiki/Zakarpattia_Oblast "Zakarpattia Oblast"), and the population became much more homogenised due to post-war population transfers, most of which, as in the case of [Germans](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_(1944%E2%80%931950) "Flight and expulsion of Germans (1944–1950)") and [Crimean Tatars](/wiki/Deportation_of_the_Crimean_Tatars "Deportation of the Crimean Tatars"), were forced. As of 1 January 1953, Ukrainians were second only to Russians among adult "[special deportees](/wiki/Forced_settlements_in_the_Soviet_Union "Forced settlements in the Soviet Union")", comprising 20% of the total.[[130]](#cite_note-Malynovska-136)

Following the death of Stalin in 1953, [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") became the new leader of the USSR, who began the policies of [de-stalinisation](/wiki/De-Stalinization "De-Stalinization") and the [Khrushchev Thaw](/wiki/Khrushchev_Thaw "Khrushchev Thaw"). During his term as head of the Soviet Union, [Crimea](/wiki/Crimean_Oblast "Crimean Oblast") was [transferred](/wiki/1954_transfer_of_Crimea "1954 transfer of Crimea") from the [Russian SFSR](/wiki/Russian_SFSR "Russian SFSR") to the [Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Ukrainian_SSR "Ukrainian SSR"), formally as a friendship gift to Ukraine and for economic reasons.[[131]](#cite_note-137) This represented the final extension of Ukrainian territory and formed the basis for the internationally recognised borders of Ukraine to this day. Many top positions in the Soviet Union were occupied by Ukrainians, including notably [Leonid Brezhnev](/wiki/Leonid_Brezhnev "Leonid Brezhnev"), [General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union") from 1964 to 1982. However, it was he and his [appointee in Ukraine](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Ukraine_(Soviet_Union) "Communist Party of Ukraine (Soviet Union)"), [Volodymyr Shcherbytsky](/wiki/Volodymyr_Shcherbytsky "Volodymyr Shcherbytsky"), who presided over the extensive [Russification](/wiki/Russification_of_Ukraine "Russification of Ukraine") of Ukraine and who were instrumental in repressing a new generation of Ukrainian intellectuals known as the [Sixtiers](/wiki/Sixtiers "Sixtiers").[[132]](#cite_note-138)

By 1950, the republic had fully surpassed pre-war levels of industry and production.[[133]](#cite_note-139) Soviet Ukraine soon became a European leader in industrial production[[134]](#cite_note-140) and an important centre of the Soviet [arms industry](/wiki/Arms_industry "Arms industry") and high-tech research, though heavy industry still had an outsided influence.[[135]](#cite_note-141) The Soviet government invested in hydroelectric and nuclear power projects to cater to the energy demand that the development carried. On 26 April 1986, however, a reactor in the [Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Chernobyl_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant") exploded, resulting in the [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster"), the worst [nuclear reactor](/wiki/Nuclear_reactor "Nuclear reactor") accident in history.[[136]](#cite_note-142)

### Independence

Further information: [Modern history of Ukraine](/wiki/Modern_history_of_Ukraine "Modern history of Ukraine"), [Dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"), [Orange Revolution](/wiki/Orange_Revolution "Orange Revolution"), [Revolution of Dignity](/wiki/Revolution_of_Dignity "Revolution of Dignity"), and [Russo-Ukrainian War](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/RIAN_archive_848095_Signing_the_Agreement_to_eliminate_the_USSR_and_establish_the_Commonwealth_of_Independent_States.jpg/250px-RIAN_archive_848095_Signing_the_Agreement_to_eliminate_the_USSR_and_establish_the_Commonwealth_of_Independent_States.jpg)](/wiki/File:RIAN_archive_848095_Signing_the_Agreement_to_eliminate_the_USSR_and_establish_the_Commonwealth_of_Independent_States.jpg)

Ukrainian president [Leonid Kravchuk](/wiki/Leonid_Kravchuk "Leonid Kravchuk") and Russian president [Boris Yeltsin](/wiki/Boris_Yeltsin "Boris Yeltsin") signing the [Belavezha Accords](/wiki/Belavezha_Accords "Belavezha Accords"), which [dissolved the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"), on 8 December 1991

[Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev") pursued a policy of limited liberalisation of public life, known as *[perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika"),* and attempted to reform a [stagnating economy](/wiki/Era_of_Stagnation "Era of Stagnation"). The latter failed, but the democratisation of the Soviet Union fuelled nationalist and separatist tendencies among the ethnic minorities, including Ukrainians.[[137]](#cite_note-143) As part of the so-called [parade of sovereignties](/wiki/Parade_of_sovereignties "Parade of sovereignties"), on 16 July 1990, the newly elected [Supreme Soviet of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Supreme_Soviet_of_the_Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Supreme Soviet of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic") adopted the [Declaration of State Sovereignty of Ukraine](/wiki/Declaration_of_State_Sovereignty_of_Ukraine "Declaration of State Sovereignty of Ukraine").[[10]](#cite_note-gska2.rada.gov.ua-14) After a [failed coup](/wiki/1991_Soviet_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_attempt "1991 Soviet coup d'état attempt") by some Communist leaders in Moscow at deposing Gorbachov, outright independence was proclaimed on 24 August 1991.[[138]](#cite_note-144) It was approved by 92% of the Ukrainian electorate in a [referendum](/wiki/1991_Ukrainian_independence_referendum "1991 Ukrainian independence referendum") on 1 December.[[139]](#cite_note-Nohlen_Stöver-145) Ukraine's new [president](/wiki/President_of_Ukraine "President of Ukraine"), Leonid Kravchuk, went on to sign the [Belavezha Accords](/wiki/Belavezha_Accords "Belavezha Accords") and made Ukraine a founding member of the much looser [Commonwealth of Independent States](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Commonwealth of Independent States") (CIS),[[140]](#cite_note-146) though Ukraine never became a full member of the latter as it did not ratify the agreement founding CIS.[[141]](#cite_note-:2-147) These documents sealed the fate of the Soviet Union, which formally voted itself out of existence on 26 December.[[142]](#cite_note-148)

Ukraine was initially viewed as having favourable economic conditions in comparison to the other regions of the Soviet Union,[[143]](#cite_note-149) though it was one of the poorer Soviet republics by the time of the dissolution.[[144]](#cite_note-Notstronk-150) However, during its transition to the market economy, the country experienced deeper economic slowdown than almost all of the other [former Soviet Republics](/wiki/Former_Soviet_Republics "Former Soviet Republics"). During the recession, between 1991 and 1999, Ukraine lost 60% of its GDP[[145]](#cite_note-IMF-151)[[146]](#cite_note-152) and suffered from [hyperinflation](/wiki/Hyperinflation "Hyperinflation") that peaked at 10,000% in 1993.[[147]](#cite_note-153) The situation only stabilised well after the new currency, the [hryvnia](/wiki/Hryvnia "Hryvnia"), fell sharply in late 1998 partially as a fallout from the [Russian debt default](/wiki/Russian_debt_default "Russian debt default") earlier that year.[[148]](#cite_note-154) The legacy of the economic policies of the nineties was the mass privatisation of state property that created a class of extremely powerful and rich individuals known as the [oligarchs](/wiki/Ukrainian_oligarch "Ukrainian oligarch").[[144]](#cite_note-Notstronk-150) The country then fell into a series of sharp recessions as a result of the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"),[[144]](#cite_note-Notstronk-150) the start of the [Russo-Ukrainian War](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War") in 2014,[[149]](#cite_note-155) and finally, the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine") by Russia on 24 February 2022.[[150]](#cite_note-156) Ukraine's economy in general underperformed since the time independence came due to pervasive [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Ukraine "Corruption in Ukraine") and mismanagement,[[151]](#cite_note-157) which, particularly in the 1990s, led to protests and organised strikes.[[152]](#cite_note-158) The war with Russia impeded meaningful economic recovery in the 2010s,[[153]](#cite_note-159) while efforts to combat the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Ukraine "COVID-19 pandemic in Ukraine"), which arrived in 2020, were made much harder by [low vaccination rates](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccine "COVID-19 vaccine")[[154]](#cite_note-160) and, later in the pandemic, by the ongoing invasion.[[155]](#cite_note-161)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Euromaidan_Kyiv_1-12-13_by_Gnatoush_009.jpg/250px-Euromaidan_Kyiv_1-12-13_by_Gnatoush_009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Euromaidan_Kyiv_1-12-13_by_Gnatoush_009.jpg)

[Euromaidan](/wiki/Euromaidan "Euromaidan") protest in Kyiv, December 2013

From the political perspective, one of the defining features of the [politics of Ukraine](/wiki/Politics_of_Ukraine "Politics of Ukraine") is that for most of the time, it has been divided along two issues: the relation between Ukraine, the [West](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") and Russia, and the classical [left-right](/wiki/Left%E2%80%93right_political_spectrum "Left–right political spectrum") divide.[[156]](#cite_note-162) The first two presidents, Kravchuk and [Leonid Kuchma](/wiki/Leonid_Kuchma "Leonid Kuchma"), tended to balance the competing visions of Ukraine,[[157]](#cite_note-163) though [Yushchenko](/wiki/Yushchenko "Yushchenko") and [Yanukovych](/wiki/Yanukovych "Yanukovych") were generally pro-Western and pro-Russian, respectively. There were two major protests against Yanukovych: the [Orange Revolution](/wiki/Orange_Revolution "Orange Revolution") in 2004, when tens of thousands of people went in protest of [election rigging](/wiki/Election_rigging "Election rigging") in his favour (Yushchenko was eventually elected president), and another one in the winter of 2013/2014, when more gathered on the [Euromaidan](/wiki/Euromaidan "Euromaidan") to oppose Yanukovych's refusal to sign the [European Union–Ukraine Association Agreement](/wiki/European_Union%E2%80%93Ukraine_Association_Agreement "European Union–Ukraine Association Agreement"). By the end of the protests on 21 February 2014, he fled from Ukraine and was removed by the parliament in what is termed the [Revolution of Dignity](/wiki/Revolution_of_Dignity "Revolution of Dignity"), but Russia refused to recognise the interim pro-Western government, calling it a *[junta](/wiki/Military_junta "Military junta")* and denouncing the events as a coup d'état sponsored by the United States.[[158]](#cite_note-164)[[159]](#cite_note-165)[[160]](#cite_note-Partido_da_imprensa_Golpista-166)

Despite the signing of the [Budapest memorandum](/wiki/Budapest_Memorandum_on_Security_Assurances "Budapest Memorandum on Security Assurances") in 1994, in which Ukraine agreed to hand over [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Ukraine_and_weapons_of_mass_destruction "Ukraine and weapons of mass destruction") in exchange for guarantees of security and territorial integrity, Russia reacted violently to these developments and [started a war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War") against its western neighbour. In late February and early March 2014, it [annexed Crimea](/wiki/Annexation_of_Crimea_by_the_Russian_Federation "Annexation of Crimea by the Russian Federation") using its [Navy](/wiki/Russian_Navy "Russian Navy") in [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol_Naval_Base "Sevastopol Naval Base") as well as the so- called [little green men](/wiki/Little_green_men_(Russo-Ukrainian_War) "Little green men (Russo-Ukrainian War)"); after this succeeded, it then launched a [proxy war in the Donbas](/wiki/War_in_Donbas_(2014%E2%80%932022) "War in Donbas (2014–2022)") via the breakaway [Donetsk People's Republic](/wiki/Donetsk_People%27s_Republic "Donetsk People's Republic") and [Luhansk People's Republic](/wiki/Luhansk_People%27s_Republic "Luhansk People's Republic").[[161]](#cite_note-167) The first months of the conflict with the Russian-backed separatists were fluid, but Russian forces then started an open invasion in Donbas on 24 August 2014. Together they pushed back Ukrainian troops to the frontline established in February 2015, i.e. after Ukrainian troops [withdrew from Debaltseve](/wiki/Battle_of_Debaltseve "Battle of Debaltseve").[[162]](#cite_note-168) It remained a [frozen conflict](/wiki/Frozen_conflict "Frozen conflict") until the early hours of 24 February 2022,[[163]](#cite_note-169) when Russia launched the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[164]](#cite_note-170) A year later, Russian troops controlled about 17% of Ukraine's internationally recognised territory, which constitutes 94% of [Luhansk Oblast](/wiki/Luhansk_Oblast "Luhansk Oblast"), 73% of [Kherson Oblast](/wiki/Kherson_Oblast "Kherson Oblast"), 72% of [Zaporizhzhia Oblast](/wiki/Zaporizhzhia_Oblast "Zaporizhzhia Oblast"), 54% of [Donetsk Oblast](/wiki/Donetsk_Oblast "Donetsk Oblast") and all of Crimea,[[165]](#cite_note-171) though Russia failed with its initial plan, with Ukrainian troops recapturing some territory in counteroffensives.[[166]](#cite_note-172)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine.svg/250px-2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine.svg)

[Russian-occupied territories of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian-occupied_territories_of_Ukraine "Russian-occupied territories of Ukraine") as of 26 March 2026

The military conflict with Russia shifted the government's policy towards the West. Shortly after Yanukovych fled Ukraine, the country signed the EU association agreement in June 2014, and its citizens were granted visa-free travel to the European Union three years later. In January 2019, the [Orthodox Church of Ukraine](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Orthodox Church of Ukraine") was recognised as independent of Moscow, which reversed the 1686 decision of the patriarch of Constantinople and dealt a further blow to Moscow's influence in Ukraine.[[167]](#cite_note-173) Finally, amid a full-scale war with Russia, Ukraine was granted [candidate status](/wiki/Potential_enlargement_of_the_European_Union "Potential enlargement of the European Union") to the European Union on 23 June 2022.[[168]](#cite_note-BBC_News-174) A broad anti-corruption drive began in early 2023 with the resignations of several deputy ministers and regional heads during a reshuffle of the government.[[169]](#cite_note-175)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Ukraine](/wiki/Geography_of_Ukraine "Geography of Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Topographic_map_of_Ukraine_%28with_borders_and_towns%29.svg/250px-Topographic_map_of_Ukraine_%28with_borders_and_towns%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Topographic_map_of_Ukraine_(with_borders_and_towns).svg)

Topographic map of Ukraine with borders and cities

Ukraine is the [second-largest European country](/wiki/List_of_European_countries_by_area "List of European countries by area"), after Russia, and the largest country entirely in Europe. Lying between latitudes [44°](/wiki/44th_parallel_north "44th parallel north") and [53° N](/wiki/53rd_parallel_north "53rd parallel north"), and longitudes [22°](/wiki/22nd_meridian_east "22nd meridian east") and [41° E](/wiki/41st_meridian_east "41st meridian east")., it is mostly in the [East European Plain](/wiki/East_European_Plain "East European Plain"). Ukraine covers an area of 603,550 square kilometres (233,030 sq mi), with a coastline of 2,782 kilometres (1,729 mi).[[43]](#cite_note-cia-47)

The landscape of Ukraine consists mostly of fertile [steppes](/wiki/Steppes "Steppes") (plains with few trees) and plateaus, crossed by rivers such as the Dnieper, [Seversky Donets](/wiki/Seversky_Donets "Seversky Donets"), Dniester and the [Southern Bug](/wiki/Southern_Bug "Southern Bug") as they flow south into the Black Sea and the smaller [Sea of Azov](/wiki/Sea_of_Azov "Sea of Azov"). To the southwest, the [Danube Delta](/wiki/Danube_Delta "Danube Delta") forms the border with Romania. Ukraine's regions have diverse geographic features, ranging from the highlands to the lowlands. The country's only mountains are the [Carpathian Mountains](/wiki/Carpathian_Mountains "Carpathian Mountains") in the west, of which the highest is [Hoverla](/wiki/Hoverla "Hoverla") at 2,061 metres (6,762 ft), and the Crimean Mountains, in the extreme south along the coast.[[170]](#cite_note-176)

Ukraine also has a number of highland regions such as the [Volyn-Podillia Upland](/wiki/Volhynian-Podolian_Upland "Volhynian-Podolian Upland") (in the west) and the Near-Dnipro Upland (on the right bank of the Dnieper). To the east there are the south-western spurs of the [Central Russian Upland](/wiki/Central_Russian_Upland "Central Russian Upland") over which runs the border with Russia. Near the Sea of Azov are the Donets Ridge and the Near Azov Upland. The [snow melt](/wiki/Snow_melt "Snow melt") from the mountains feeds the rivers and their [waterfalls](/wiki/Waterfalls_of_Ukraine "Waterfalls of Ukraine").

Significant natural resources in Ukraine include [lithium](/wiki/Lithium "Lithium"),[[171]](#cite_note-177) natural gas,[[172]](#cite_note-:0-178) [kaolin](/wiki/Kaolin "Kaolin"),[[172]](#cite_note-:0-178) timber[[173]](#cite_note-179) and an abundance of [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land").[[174]](#cite_note-180) Ukraine has many environmental issues.[[175]](#cite_note-181)[[176]](#cite_note-182) Some regions lack adequate supplies of potable water.[[177]](#cite_note-183) Air and water pollution affects the country, as well as deforestation, and radiation contamination in the northeast from the 1986 accident at the [Chernobyl](/wiki/Chernobyl "Chernobyl") Nuclear Power Plant.[[178]](#cite_note-184) The environmental damage caused by the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine") has been described as an [ecocide](/wiki/Ecocide "Ecocide"),[[179]](#cite_note-185) the [destruction](/wiki/Destruction_of_the_Kakhovka_Dam "Destruction of the Kakhovka Dam") of [Kakhovka Dam](/wiki/Kakhovka_Dam "Kakhovka Dam"), severe pollution and millions of tonnes of contaminated debris is estimated to cost over [USD](/wiki/USD "USD") 50 billion to repair.[[180]](#cite_note-pax-186)[[181]](#cite_note-enviroyale-187)[[182]](#cite_note-188)[[183]](#cite_note-189)[[184]](#cite_note-190)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Koppen-Geiger_Map_UKR_present.svg/330px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_UKR_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_UKR_present.svg)

[Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") map of Ukraine

Ukraine is in the mid-latitudes, and generally has a [continental climate](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate"), except for its southern coasts, which have [cold semi-arid](/wiki/Cold_semi-arid_climate "Cold semi-arid climate") and [humid subtropical climates](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate").[[185]](#cite_note-faoclimate-191) Average annual temperatures range from 5.5–7 °C (41.9–44.6 °F) in the north, to 11–13 °C (51.8–55.4 °F) in the south.[[186]](#cite_note-ebclimate-192) [Precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") is highest in the west and north and lowest in the east and southeast.[[186]](#cite_note-ebclimate-192) Western Ukraine, particularly in the Carpathian Mountains, receives around 120 centimetres (47.2 in) of precipitation annually, while Crimea and the coastal areas of the Black Sea receive around 40 centimetres (15.7 in).[[186]](#cite_note-ebclimate-192)

Water availability from the major river basins is expected to decrease [due to climate change](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change "Effects of climate change"), especially in summer. This poses risks to the agricultural sector.[[187]](#cite_note-193) The negative [impacts of climate change on agriculture](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change_on_agriculture "Effects of climate change on agriculture") are mostly felt in the south of the country, which has a [steppe](/wiki/Steppe "Steppe") climate. In the north, some crops may be able to benefit from a longer growing season.[[188]](#cite_note-194) The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") has stated that Ukraine is highly [vulnerable to climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_vulnerability "Climate change vulnerability").[[189]](#cite_note-195)
