[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/WLE_-_2020_-_%D0%90%D0%B9-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D1%8F%D0%B9%D0%BB%D0%B0.jpg/250px-WLE_-_2020_-_%D0%90%D0%B9-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D1%8F%D0%B9%D0%BB%D0%B0.jpg)](/wiki/File:WLE_-_2020_-_%D0%90%D0%B9-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D1%8F%D0%B9%D0%BB%D0%B0.jpg)

View from the western slope of Mount Ai-Petri of the [Ai-Petri](/wiki/Ai-Petri "Ai-Petri") plateau, in Crimea designated by the Ukrainian government as a natural heritage site

Ukraine contains six terrestrial [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion"): [Central European mixed forests](/wiki/Central_European_mixed_forests "Central European mixed forests"), [Crimean Submediterranean forest complex](/wiki/Crimean_Submediterranean_forest_complex "Crimean Submediterranean forest complex"), [East European forest steppe](/wiki/East_European_forest_steppe "East European forest steppe"), [Pannonian mixed forests](/wiki/Pannonian_mixed_forests "Pannonian mixed forests"), [Carpathian montane conifer forests](/wiki/Carpathian_montane_conifer_forests "Carpathian montane conifer forests"), and Pontic steppe.[[190]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-196) There is somewhat more [coniferous](/wiki/Conifer "Conifer") than [deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") forest.[[191]](#cite_note-ShvidenkoBukshaKrakovska2017-197) The most densely forested area is [Polisia](/wiki/Polisia "Polisia") in the northwest, with pine, oak, and birch.[[191]](#cite_note-ShvidenkoBukshaKrakovska2017-197) There are 45,000 species of animals (mostly invertebrates),[[192]](#cite_note-Conference2001-198) with approximately 385 endangered species listed in the [Red Data Book of Ukraine](/wiki/Red_Data_Book_of_Ukraine "Red Data Book of Ukraine").[[193]](#cite_note-State-199) [Internationally important wetlands](/wiki/Ramsar_site "Ramsar site") cover over 7,000 square kilometres (2,700 sq mi), with the Danube Delta being important for conservation.[[194]](#cite_note-wetland-200)[[195]](#cite_note-Ramsar-201)

## Politics

Main article: [Politics of Ukraine](/wiki/Politics_of_Ukraine "Politics of Ukraine")

Ukraine is a republic under a [semi-presidential system](/wiki/Semi-presidential_system "Semi-presidential system") with separate [legislative](/wiki/Legislative_branch "Legislative branch"), [executive](/wiki/Executive_branch "Executive branch"), and [judicial branches](/wiki/Judicial_branch "Judicial branch").[[196]](#cite_note-Choudhry_2018_p16.-202)

### Constitution

Main article: [Constitution of Ukraine](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ukraine "Constitution of Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Chart_Constitution_of_Ukraine_EN.svg/330px-Chart_Constitution_of_Ukraine_EN.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Chart_Constitution_of_Ukraine_EN.svg)

Chart of the political system of Ukraine

The Constitution of Ukraine was adopted and ratified at the 5th session of the [Verkhovna Rada](/wiki/Verkhovna_Rada "Verkhovna Rada"), the parliament of Ukraine, on 28 June 1996.[[197]](#cite_note-UNIANCD28616-203) The constitution was passed with 315 ayes out of 450 votes possible (300 ayes minimum).[[197]](#cite_note-UNIANCD28616-203) All other laws and other normative[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] legal acts of Ukraine must conform to the constitution. The right to amend the constitution through a special legislative procedure is vested exclusively in the parliament. The only body that may interpret the constitution and determine whether legislation conforms to it is the [Constitutional Court of Ukraine](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Ukraine "Constitutional Court of Ukraine"). Since 1996, the [public holiday](/wiki/Public_holiday "Public holiday") [Constitution Day](/wiki/Constitution_Day_(Ukraine) "Constitution Day (Ukraine)") is celebrated on 28 June.[[198]](#cite_note-204)[[199]](#cite_note-UW29121996-205) On 7 February 2019, the Verkhovna Rada voted to amend the constitution to state Ukraine's strategic objectives as joining the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO").[[200]](#cite_note-206)

### Government

Main article: [Government of Ukraine](/wiki/Government_of_Ukraine "Government of Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Volodymyr_Zelensky_2022_official_portrait_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Volodymyr_Zelensky_2022_official_portrait_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Volodymyr_Zelensky_2022_official_portrait_(cropped).jpg)

[Volodymyr Zelensky](/wiki/Volodymyr_Zelensky "Volodymyr Zelensky")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_Ukraine "President of Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/%D0%A1%D0%92%D0%98%D0%A0%D0%98%D0%94%D0%95%D0%9D%D0%9A%D0%9E_%D0%AE%D0%BB%D1%96%D1%8F_%D0%90%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%96%D1%97%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B0_%28cropped_2%29.jpg/250px-%D0%A1%D0%92%D0%98%D0%A0%D0%98%D0%94%D0%95%D0%9D%D0%9A%D0%9E_%D0%AE%D0%BB%D1%96%D1%8F_%D0%90%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%96%D1%97%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B0_%28cropped_2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A1%D0%92%D0%98%D0%A0%D0%98%D0%94%D0%95%D0%9D%D0%9A%D0%9E_%D0%AE%D0%BB%D1%96%D1%8F_%D0%90%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%96%D1%97%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B0_(cropped_2).jpg)

[Yulia Svyrydenko](/wiki/Yulia_Svyrydenko "Yulia Svyrydenko")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Ukraine "Prime Minister of Ukraine")

The president is elected by popular vote for a five-year term and is the formal [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state").[[201]](#cite_note-207)
Ukraine's legislative branch includes the 450-seat [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") parliament, the Verkhovna Rada.[[202]](#cite_note-208) The parliament is primarily responsible for the formation of the executive branch and the [Cabinet of Ministers](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Ministers_of_Ukraine "Cabinet of Ministers of Ukraine"), headed by the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Ukraine "Prime Minister of Ukraine").[[203]](#cite_note-209) The president retains the authority to nominate the ministers of foreign affairs and of defence for parliamentary approval, as well as the power to appoint the [prosecutor general](/wiki/Prosecutor_General_of_Ukraine "Prosecutor General of Ukraine") and the head of the [Security Service](/wiki/Security_Service_of_Ukraine "Security Service of Ukraine").[[204]](#cite_note-210)

Laws, acts of the parliament and the cabinet, presidential decrees, and acts of the [Crimean parliament](/wiki/Verkhovna_Rada_of_Crimea "Verkhovna Rada of Crimea") may be abrogated by the Constitutional Court, should they be found to violate the constitution. Other normative acts are subject to judicial review. The [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Ukraine "Supreme Court of Ukraine") is the main body in the system of courts of general jurisdiction.
Local self-government is officially guaranteed. Local councils and city mayors are popularly elected and exercise control over local budgets. The heads of regional and district administrations are appointed by the president in accordance with proposals of the prime minister.[[205]](#cite_note-House2004-211)

### Courts and law enforcement

Main articles: [Judicial system of Ukraine](/wiki/Judicial_system_of_Ukraine "Judicial system of Ukraine") and [Law enforcement in Ukraine](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Ukraine "Law enforcement in Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Klov_Palace._Listed_ID_80-382-0462._-_8_Pylypa_Orlyka_Street%2C_Pechersk_Raion%2C_Kiev._-_Pechersk_28_09_13_396.jpg/250px-Klov_Palace._Listed_ID_80-382-0462._-_8_Pylypa_Orlyka_Street%2C_Pechersk_Raion%2C_Kiev._-_Pechersk_28_09_13_396.jpg)](/wiki/File:Klov_Palace._Listed_ID_80-382-0462._-_8_Pylypa_Orlyka_Street,_Pechersk_Raion,_Kiev._-_Pechersk_28_09_13_396.jpg)

[Klovsky Palace](/wiki/Klov_Palace "Klov Palace"), seat of the [Supreme Court of Ukraine](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Ukraine "Supreme Court of Ukraine")

[Martial law](/wiki/Martial_law_in_Ukraine "Martial law in Ukraine") was declared when Russia invaded in February 2022,[[206]](#cite_note-212) and continues.[[207]](#cite_note-213)[[208]](#cite_note-214) The courts enjoy legal, financial and constitutional freedom guaranteed by Ukrainian law since 2002. Judges are largely well protected from dismissal (except for gross misconduct). Court justices are appointed by presidential decree for an initial period of five years, after which Ukraine's Supreme Council confirms their positions for life. Although there are still problems, the system is considered to have been much improved since Ukraine's independence in 1991. The Supreme Court is regarded as an independent and impartial body, and has on several occasions ruled against the Ukrainian government. The [World Justice Project](/wiki/World_Justice_Project "World Justice Project") ranks Ukraine 66 out of 99 countries surveyed in its annual Rule of Law Index.[[209]](#cite_note-215)

[Prosecutors](/wiki/Prosecutor "Prosecutor") in Ukraine have greater powers than in most European countries, and according to the [European Commission for Democracy through Law](/wiki/European_Commission_for_Democracy_through_Law "European Commission for Democracy through Law") "the role and functions of the Prosecutor's Office is not in accordance with [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") standards".[[210]](#cite_note-216) The [conviction rate](/wiki/Conviction_rate "Conviction rate") is over 99%,[[211]](#cite_note-217) equal to the conviction rate of the Soviet Union, with suspects often being incarcerated for long periods before trial.[[212]](#cite_note-:7-218)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/%D0%91%D1%83%D0%B4%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%BA_%D1%83%D1%80%D1%8F%D0%B4%D1%83_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D0%B8%2C_%D0%9A%D0%B8%D1%97%D0%B2.JPG/250px-%D0%91%D1%83%D0%B4%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%BA_%D1%83%D1%80%D1%8F%D0%B4%D1%83_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D0%B8%2C_%D0%9A%D0%B8%D1%97%D0%B2.JPG)](/wiki/File:%D0%91%D1%83%D0%B4%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%BA_%D1%83%D1%80%D1%8F%D0%B4%D1%83_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D0%B8,_%D0%9A%D0%B8%D1%97%D0%B2.JPG)

The [Cabinet of Ministers](/wiki/Government_of_Ukraine "Government of Ukraine") building

In 2010, [President Yanukovych](/wiki/Viktor_Yanukovych "Viktor Yanukovych") formed an expert group to make recommendations on how to "clean up the current mess and adopt a law on court organisation".[[212]](#cite_note-:7-218) One day later, he stated "We can no longer disgrace our country with such a court system."[[212]](#cite_note-:7-218) The criminal judicial system and the prison system of Ukraine remain quite punitive.[[213]](#cite_note-United_States_Department_of_State_2021-219)

Since 2010 court proceedings can be held in Russian by mutual consent of the parties. Citizens unable to speak Ukrainian or Russian may use their native language or the services of a translator.[[214]](#cite_note-220)[[215]](#cite_note-221) Previously all court proceedings had to be held in Ukrainian.[[213]](#cite_note-United_States_Department_of_State_2021-219)

Law enforcement agencies are controlled by the [Ministry of Internal Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Internal_Affairs_of_Ukraine "Ministry of Internal Affairs of Ukraine"). They consist primarily of the [national police force](/wiki/National_Police_of_Ukraine "National Police of Ukraine") and various specialised units and agencies such as the [State Border Guard](/wiki/State_Border_Guard_Service_of_Ukraine "State Border Guard Service of Ukraine") and the [Coast Guard](/wiki/Ukrainian_Sea_Guard "Ukrainian Sea Guard") services. Law enforcement agencies, particularly the police, faced criticism for their heavy handling of the 2004 Orange Revolution. Many thousands of police officers were stationed throughout the capital, primarily to dissuade protesters from challenging the state's authority but also to provide a quick reaction force in case of need; most officers were armed.[[216]](#cite_note-NYTSBU-222)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Administrative divisions of Ukraine](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Ukraine "Administrative divisions of Ukraine"), [Ukrainian historical regions](/wiki/Ukrainian_historical_regions "Ukrainian historical regions"), and [List of cities in Ukraine](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Ukraine "List of cities in Ukraine")

Further information: [Political status of Crimea](/wiki/Political_status_of_Crimea "Political status of Crimea") and [Russian-occupied territories](/wiki/Russian-occupied_territories "Russian-occupied territories")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Map_of_Ukraine_with_Cities.png/500px-Map_of_Ukraine_with_Cities.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Ukraine_with_Cities.png)

Ukraine (2021) — major cities and adjacent countries

The system of Ukrainian subdivisions reflects the country's status as a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") (as stated in the country's constitution) with unified legal and [administrative](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") regimes for each unit.

Including [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol "Sevastopol") and the [Autonomous Republic of Crimea](/wiki/Autonomous_Republic_of_Crimea "Autonomous Republic of Crimea") that were annexed by the Russian Federation in 2014, Ukraine consists of 27 regions: twenty-four [oblasts](/wiki/Oblast "Oblast") (provinces), one [autonomous republic](/wiki/Autonomous_republic "Autonomous republic") ([Autonomous Republic of Crimea](/wiki/Autonomous_Republic_of_Crimea "Autonomous Republic of Crimea")), and two cities of special status—[Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), the capital, and [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol "Sevastopol"). The 24 oblasts and Crimea are subdivided into 136 *[raions](/wiki/Raion "Raion")* (districts) and city municipalities of regional significance, or second-level administrative units.[[217]](#cite_note-223)

[Populated places in Ukraine](/wiki/Populated_places_in_Ukraine "Populated places in Ukraine") are split into two categories: urban and rural. Urban populated places are split further into cities and [urban-type settlements](/wiki/Urban-type_settlement "Urban-type settlement") (a Soviet administrative invention), while rural populated places consist of villages and settlements (a generally used term). All cities have a certain degree of self-rule depending on their significance such as national significance (as in the case of Kyiv and Sevastopol), regional significance (within each oblast or autonomous republic) or district significance (all the rest of cities). A city's significance depends on several factors such as its population, socio-economic and historical importance and infrastructure.

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Ukraine](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Ukraine "Foreign relations of Ukraine")

See also: [International membership of Ukraine](/wiki/International_membership_of_Ukraine "International membership of Ukraine"), [Ukraine–European Union relations](/wiki/Ukraine%E2%80%93European_Union_relations "Ukraine–European Union relations"), [Accession of Ukraine to the European Union](/wiki/Accession_of_Ukraine_to_the_European_Union "Accession of Ukraine to the European Union"), [Ukraine and the World Bank](/wiki/Ukraine_and_the_World_Bank "Ukraine and the World Bank"), and [Ukrainian nationality law](/wiki/Ukrainian_nationality_law "Ukrainian nationality law")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Batumi_International_Conference%2C_on_19_July_2021_03_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Batumi_International_Conference%2C_on_19_July_2021_03_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Batumi_International_Conference,_on_19_July_2021_03_(cropped).jpg)

[President of Georgia](/wiki/President_of_Georgia "President of Georgia") [Salome Zurabishvili](/wiki/Salome_Zourabichvili "Salome Zourabichvili"), [President of Moldova](/wiki/President_of_Moldova "President of Moldova") [Maia Sandu](/wiki/Maia_Sandu "Maia Sandu"), [Ukrainian President](/wiki/President_of_Ukraine "President of Ukraine") [Volodymyr Zelenskyy](/wiki/Volodymyr_Zelenskyy "Volodymyr Zelenskyy") and [European Council President](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Council "President of the European Council") [Charles Michel](/wiki/Charles_Michel "Charles Michel") during the 2021 International Conference in [Batumi](/wiki/Batumi "Batumi"). In 2014, the EU signed association agreements with all three countries

From 1999 to 2001, Ukraine served as a non-permanent member of the [UN Security Council](/wiki/UN_Security_Council "UN Security Council"). Historically, Soviet Ukraine joined the United Nations in 1945 as one of the original members following a Western compromise with the Soviet Union.[[218]](#cite_note-U.S._Department_of_State,_Bureau_of_Public_Affairs,_Office_of_Public_Communication,_Editorial_Division.2000-224) Ukraine has consistently supported peaceful, negotiated settlements to disputes. It has participated in the quadripartite talks on the conflict in Moldova and promoted a peaceful resolution to the conflict in the [post-Soviet state](/wiki/Post-Soviet_states "Post-Soviet states") of Georgia. Ukraine also has made contributions to UN [peacekeeping](/wiki/Peacekeeping "Peacekeeping") operations since 1992.[[219]](#cite_note-NATO_Information_Service.-225)

Ukraine considers Euro-Atlantic integration its primary foreign policy objective,[[220]](#cite_note-result_of_Russia-226) but in practice it has always balanced its relationship with the European Union and the United States with strong ties to Russia. The European Union's [Partnership and Cooperation Agreement](/wiki/Partnership_and_Cooperation_Agreement "Partnership and Cooperation Agreement") (PCA) with Ukraine went into force in 1998. The European Union (EU) has encouraged Ukraine to implement the PCA fully before discussions begin on an association agreement, issued at the EU Summit in December 1999 in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), recognises Ukraine's long-term aspirations but does not discuss association.[[220]](#cite_note-result_of_Russia-226)

In 1992, Ukraine joined the then-Conference on Security and Cooperation in Europe (now the [Organisation for Security and Cooperation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Cooperation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe") (OSCE)), and also became a member of the [North Atlantic Cooperation Council](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Cooperation_Council "North Atlantic Cooperation Council"). [Ukraine–NATO relations](/wiki/Ukraine%E2%80%93NATO_relations "Ukraine–NATO relations") are close and the country has declared interest in eventual membership.[[220]](#cite_note-result_of_Russia-226)

Ukraine is the most active member of the [Partnership for Peace](/wiki/Partnership_for_Peace "Partnership for Peace") (PfP). All major political parties in Ukraine support full eventual integration into the European Union.[[221]](#cite_note-227) The Association Agreement between Ukraine and the European Union was signed in 2014.[[222]](#cite_note-228) Ukraine long had close ties with all its neighbours, but [Russia–Ukraine relations](/wiki/Russia%E2%80%93Ukraine_relations "Russia–Ukraine relations") rapidly deteriorated in 2014 due to the annexation of Crimea, energy dependence and payment disputes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/EU_DCFTA_EFTA.svg/250px-EU_DCFTA_EFTA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:EU_DCFTA_EFTA.svg)

In January 2016, Ukraine joined   the [Deep and Comprehensive Free Trade Area](/wiki/Deep_and_Comprehensive_Free_Trade_Area "Deep and Comprehensive Free Trade Area") with   the EU, established by the [Ukraine–European Union Association Agreement](/wiki/Ukraine%E2%80%93European_Union_Association_Agreement "Ukraine–European Union Association Agreement"), opening its path towards [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration")

The [Deep and Comprehensive Free Trade Area](/wiki/Deep_and_Comprehensive_Free_Trade_Area "Deep and Comprehensive Free Trade Area") (DCFTA), which entered into force in January 2016 following the ratification of the [Ukraine–European Union Association Agreement](/wiki/Ukraine%E2%80%93European_Union_Association_Agreement "Ukraine–European Union Association Agreement"), formally integrates Ukraine into the [European Single Market](/wiki/European_Single_Market "European Single Market") and the [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area").[[223]](#cite_note-229)[[224]](#cite_note-230) Ukraine receives further support and assistance for its [EU-accession](/wiki/Future_enlargement_of_the_European_Union "Future enlargement of the European Union") aspirations from the International Visegrád Fund of the [Visegrád Group](/wiki/Visegr%C3%A1d_Group "Visegrád Group") that consists of [Central European](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") [EU members](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union") the Czech Republic, Poland, Hungary and Slovakia.[[225]](#cite_note-231)

In 2020, in [Lublin](/wiki/Lublin "Lublin"), Lithuania, Poland and Ukraine created the [Lublin Triangle](/wiki/Lublin_Triangle "Lublin Triangle") initiative, which aims to create further cooperation between the three historical countries of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth and further Ukraine's integration and accession to the [EU](/wiki/EU "EU") and NATO.[[226]](#cite_note-232)

In 2021, the [Association Trio](/wiki/Association_Trio "Association Trio") was formed by signing a joint memorandum between the [Foreign Ministers of Georgia](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_of_Georgia "Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Georgia"), [Moldova](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_and_European_Integration_of_Moldova "Ministry of Foreign Affairs and European Integration of Moldova") and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Ukraine) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Ukraine)"). The Association Trio is a tripartite format for enhanced cooperation, coordination, and dialogue between the three countries (that have signed the Association Agreement with the EU) with the European Union on issues of common interest related to [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration"), enhancing cooperation within the framework of the [Eastern Partnership](/wiki/Eastern_Partnership "Eastern Partnership"), and committing to the prospect of joining the European Union.[[227]](#cite_note-233) As of 2021, Ukraine was preparing to formally apply for EU membership in 2024, in order to join the European Union in the 2030s,[[228]](#cite_note-:1-234) however, with the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy requested that the country be admitted to the EU immediately.[[229]](#cite_note-auto1-235)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Candidate status was granted in June 2022.[[168]](#cite_note-BBC_News-174) In recent years, Ukraine has dramatically strengthened its ties with the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[13]](#cite_note-:6-17)[[12]](#cite_note-:5-16)

In June 2025, Ukraine legalised multiple citizenship.[[230]](#cite_note-U24MediaPassports-236)[[231]](#cite_note-KyivIndPassports-237)

### Military

Main article: [Armed Forces of Ukraine](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_Ukraine "Armed Forces of Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/UA_25th_brigade_BMP-1TS_02.jpg/330px-UA_25th_brigade_BMP-1TS_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:UA_25th_brigade_BMP-1TS_02.jpg)

Ukrainian troops on the move during the [2022 Ukrainian eastern counteroffensive](/wiki/2022_Ukrainian_eastern_counteroffensive "2022 Ukrainian eastern counteroffensive")

After the dissolution of the Soviet Union, Ukraine inherited a 780,000-man military force on its territory, equipped with the third-largest [nuclear weapons arsenal](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_and_Ukraine "Nuclear weapons and Ukraine") in the world.[[232]](#cite_note-milgov-238)[[233]](#cite_note-239) In 1992, Ukraine signed the [Lisbon Protocol](/wiki/Lisbon_Protocol "Lisbon Protocol") in which the country agreed to give up all nuclear weapons to Russia for disposal and to join the [Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty](/wiki/Nuclear_Non-Proliferation_Treaty "Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty") as a non-nuclear weapon state. By 1996 the country had become free of nuclear weapons.[[232]](#cite_note-milgov-238)

Ukraine took consistent steps toward reduction of conventional weapons. It signed the [Treaty on Conventional Armed Forces in Europe](/wiki/Treaty_on_Conventional_Armed_Forces_in_Europe "Treaty on Conventional Armed Forces in Europe"), which called for reduction of tanks, artillery, and armoured vehicles (army forces were reduced to 300,000). The country planned to convert the current [conscript](/wiki/Conscript "Conscript")-based military into a professional [volunteer military](/wiki/Volunteer_military "Volunteer military").[[234]](#cite_note-wbook06-240)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Ukraine's current military consist of 196,600 active personnel and around 900,000 reservists.[[235]](#cite_note-241)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Ukrainian_HIMARS_in_Zaporizhya_oblast%2C_early_June_2022_%283%29.jpg/330px-Ukrainian_HIMARS_in_Zaporizhya_oblast%2C_early_June_2022_%283%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ukrainian_HIMARS_in_Zaporizhya_oblast,_early_June_2022_(3).jpg)

American [M142 HIMARS](/wiki/M142_HIMARS "M142 HIMARS") rocket launchers in Ukrainian service, an example of [foreign military equipment received](/wiki/List_of_military_aid_to_Ukraine_during_the_Russo-Ukrainian_War "List of military aid to Ukraine during the Russo-Ukrainian War") during the [Russo-Ukrainian War](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War")

Ukraine played an increasing role in peacekeeping operations. In 2014, the Ukrainian frigate *Hetman Sagaidachniy* joined the European Union's counter-piracy [Operation Atalanta](/wiki/Operation_Atalanta "Operation Atalanta") and was part of the EU Naval Force off the coast of [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia") for two months.[[236]](#cite_note-242) Ukrainian troops were deployed in [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo") as part of the [Ukrainian-Polish Battalion](/wiki/Polish-Ukrainian_Peace_Force_Battalion "Polish-Ukrainian Peace Force Battalion").[[237]](#cite_note-243) In 2003–2005, a Ukrainian unit was deployed as part of the [multinational force in Iraq](/wiki/Multinational_force_in_Iraq "Multinational force in Iraq") under Polish command.[[238]](#cite_note-244) Military units of other states participated in multinational military exercises with Ukrainian forces in Ukraine regularly, including [U.S. military](/wiki/U.S._military "U.S. military") forces.[[239]](#cite_note-245)

Following independence, Ukraine declared itself a neutral state.[[10]](#cite_note-gska2.rada.gov.ua-14) The country had a limited military partnership with Russian Federation and other CIS countries and has had a partnership with NATO since 1994. In the 2000s, the government was leaning towards NATO, and deeper cooperation with the alliance was set by the NATO-Ukraine Action Plan signed in 2002. It was later agreed that the question of joining NATO should be answered by a national referendum at some point in the future.[[234]](#cite_note-wbook06-240) Deposed President Viktor Yanukovych considered the then level of co-operation between Ukraine and NATO sufficient, and was against Ukraine joining NATO. During the [2008 Bucharest summit](/wiki/2008_Bucharest_summit "2008 Bucharest summit"), NATO declared that Ukraine would eventually become a member of NATO when it meets the criteria for accession.

As part of its modernisation after the beginning of the [Russo-Ukrainian War](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War") in 2014, junior officers were allowed to take more initiative and a [territorial defence force](/wiki/Territorial_defence_battalions_(Ukraine) "Territorial defence battalions (Ukraine)") of volunteers was established.[[240]](#cite_note-246) Various defensive weapons including [drones](/wiki/Unmanned_combat_aerial_vehicle "Unmanned combat aerial vehicle") were supplied by many countries, but not fighter jets.[[241]](#cite_note-247) During the first few weeks of the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine") the military found it difficult to defend against shelling, missiles and high level bombing; but light infantry used shoulder-mounted weapons effectively to destroy tanks, armoured vehicles and low-flying aircraft.[[242]](#cite_note-248) In August 2023, the U.S. officials estimated that up to 70,000 Ukrainian soldiers were killed and 100,000 to 120,000 wounded during the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)").[[243]](#cite_note-249)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Ukraine](/wiki/Economy_of_Ukraine "Economy of Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/%D0%9C%D1%96%D1%81%D1%82_%D0%9F%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%B7_%D0%BD%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%85%D1%96%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%82%D1%83%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%B4%D1%81%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%82%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D1%82%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B0_%D0%9B%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%B1%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B3%D0%B0.jpg/250px-%D0%9C%D1%96%D1%81%D1%82_%D0%9F%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%B7_%D0%BD%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%85%D1%96%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%82%D1%83%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%B4%D1%81%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%82%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D1%82%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B0_%D0%9B%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%B1%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B3%D0%B0.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9C%D1%96%D1%81%D1%82_%D0%9F%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%B7_%D0%BD%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%85%D1%96%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%82%D1%83%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%B4%D1%81%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%82%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D1%82%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B0_%D0%9B%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%B1%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B3%D0%B0.jpg)

[Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), the [financial centre](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre") of Ukraine

In 2021, agriculture was the biggest sector of the economy. Ukraine is one of the world's [largest wheat exporters](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_wheat_exports "List of countries by wheat exports"). It remains among the [poorest countries in Europe](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_in_Europe_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of sovereign states in Europe by GDP (nominal) per capita") with the lowest [nominal GDP per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita").[[244]](#cite_note-poor-250) Despite improvements, as in Moldova [corruption in Ukraine](/wiki/Corruption_in_Ukraine "Corruption in Ukraine") remains an obstacle to [joining the EU](/wiki/Accession_of_Ukraine_to_the_European_Union "Accession of Ukraine to the European Union"); the country was rated 104th out of 181 in the [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index") for 2025.[[245]](#cite_note-251) In 2021, Ukraine's [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") per capita by [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") was just over $14,000.[[246]](#cite_note-252) Despite supplying [emergency financial support](/wiki/List_of_military_aid_to_Ukraine_during_the_Russo-Ukrainian_War "List of military aid to Ukraine during the Russo-Ukrainian War"), the [IMF](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") expected the economy to shrink considerably by 35% in 2022 due to the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)").[[247]](#cite_note-auto4-253) One 2022 estimate was that post-war reconstruction costs might reach half a trillion dollars.[[248]](#cite_note-254)

In 2021, the average salary in Ukraine reached its highest level at almost [₴](/wiki/Ukrainian_hryvnia "Ukrainian hryvnia")14,300 (US$525) per month.[[249]](#cite_note-255) About 1% of Ukrainians lived [below the national poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_by_country "Poverty by country") in 2019.[[250]](#cite_note-256) Unemployment in Ukraine was 4.5% in 2019.[[251]](#cite_note-257) In 2019 5–15% of the Ukrainian population were categorised as middle class.[[252]](#cite_note-258) In 2020 Ukraine's [government debt](/wiki/Government_debt "Government debt") was roughly 50% of its nominal GDP.[[253]](#cite_note-259)[[254]](#cite_note-:4-260)

In 2021 mineral commodities and light industry were important sectors.[[254]](#cite_note-:4-260) Ukraine produces nearly all types of transportation vehicles and [spacecraft](/wiki/State_Space_Agency_of_Ukraine "State Space Agency of Ukraine").[[255]](#cite_note-261)[[256]](#cite_note-262)[[257]](#cite_note-263) The [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") is the country's main trade partner, and remittances from Ukrainians working abroad are important.[[254]](#cite_note-:4-260)

### Agriculture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Spasiv_Rivne_Oblast_Ukraine_4.jpg/250px-Spasiv_Rivne_Oblast_Ukraine_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spasiv_Rivne_Oblast_Ukraine_4.jpg)

Wheat crop in Spasiv village, Rivne Oblast, Ukraine.

Ukraine is among the world's top agricultural producers and exporters and is often described as the "bread basket of Europe". During the 2020/21 international wheat marketing season (July–June), it ranked as the sixth largest wheat exporter, accounting for nine percent of world wheat trade.[[258]](#cite_note-:02-264) The country is also a major global exporter of [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize"), barley and rapeseed. In 2020/21, it accounted for 12 percent of global trade in [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") and [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley") and for 14 percent of world [rapeseed](/wiki/Rapeseed "Rapeseed") exports. Its trade share is even greater in the sunflower oil sector, with the country accounting for about 50 percent of world exports in 2020/2021.[[258]](#cite_note-:02-264)

According to the [Food and Agriculture Organisation of the United Nations](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization "Food and Agriculture Organization") (FAO), further to causing the loss of lives and increasing humanitarian needs, the likely disruptions caused by the [Russo-Ukrainian War](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War") to Ukraine's grain and oilseed sectors, could jeopardise the food security of many countries, especially those that are highly dependent on Ukraine and Russia for their food and fertiliser imports.[[259]](#cite_note-265) Several of these countries fall into the [Least Developed Country](/wiki/Least_developed_countries "Least developed countries") (LDC) group, while many others belong to the group of [Low-Income Food-Deficit Countries](/w/index.php?title=Low-Income_Food-Deficit_Countries&action=edit&redlink=1 "Low-Income Food-Deficit Countries (page does not exist)") (LIFDCs).[[260]](#cite_note-266)[[261]](#cite_note-267) For example [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea") sourced 47 percent of its wheat imports in 2021 from Ukraine. Overall, more than 30 nations depend on Ukraine and the Russian Federation for over 30 percent of their wheat import needs, many of them in North Africa and Western and Central Asia.[[258]](#cite_note-:02-264)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Ukraine](/wiki/Tourism_in_Ukraine "Tourism in Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/%D0%97%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0_%D1%84%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%86%D1%8F.jpg/250px-%D0%97%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0_%D1%84%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%86%D1%8F.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%97%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0_%D1%84%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%86%D1%8F.jpg)

[Kamianets-Podilskyi Castle](/wiki/Kamianets-Podilskyi_Castle "Kamianets-Podilskyi Castle"), one of the [Seven Wonders of Ukraine](/wiki/Seven_Wonders_of_Ukraine "Seven Wonders of Ukraine")

Before the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War") the number of tourists visiting Ukraine was eighth in Europe, according to [UN Tourism](/wiki/UN_Tourism "UN Tourism") [rankings](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings").[[262]](#cite_note-268) Ukraine has numerous tourist attractions: mountain ranges suitable for [skiing](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing"), hiking and fishing; the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") coastline as a popular summer destination; [nature reserves](/wiki/Nature_reserve "Nature reserve") of different [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem"); and churches, [castle](/wiki/Castle "Castle") ruins and other architectural and park landmarks. [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv"), [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa "Odesa") and [Kamianets-Podilskyi](/wiki/Kamianets-Podilskyi "Kamianets-Podilskyi") were Ukraine's principal tourist centres, each offering many historical landmarks and extensive [hospitality](/wiki/Hospitality "Hospitality") infrastructure. The [Seven Wonders of Ukraine](/wiki/Seven_Wonders_of_Ukraine "Seven Wonders of Ukraine") and [Seven Natural Wonders of Ukraine](/wiki/Seven_Natural_Wonders_of_Ukraine "Seven Natural Wonders of Ukraine") are selections of the most important landmarks of Ukraine, chosen by Ukrainian experts and an Internet-based public vote. Tourism was the mainstay of Crimea's economy before a major fall in visitor numbers following the Russian annexation in 2014.[[263]](#cite_note-269)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Ukraine](/wiki/Transport_in_Ukraine "Transport in Ukraine")

[![HRCS2 unit](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/%D0%A1%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B5%D0%B7%D0%B4_%22%D0%A5%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%B0%D0%B9%22.jpg/250px-%D0%A1%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B5%D0%B7%D0%B4_%22%D0%A5%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%B0%D0%B9%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A1%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B5%D0%B7%D0%B4_%22%D0%A5%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%B0%D0%B9%22.jpg)

[HRCS2 multiple unit](/wiki/HRCS2_multiple_unit "HRCS2 multiple unit"). [Rail transport](/wiki/Ukrainian_Railways "Ukrainian Railways") is heavily utilised in Ukraine.

Many roads and bridges were destroyed, and international maritime travel was blocked by the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)").[[247]](#cite_note-auto4-253) Before that it was mainly through the [Port of Odesa](/wiki/Port_of_Odesa "Port of Odesa"), from where ferries sailed regularly to [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), [Varna](/wiki/Varna,_Bulgaria "Varna, Bulgaria") and [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa"). The largest ferry company operating these routes was [Ukrferry](/wiki/UkrFerry "UkrFerry").[[264]](#cite_note-270) There are over 1,600 km (1,000 mi) of [navigable](/wiki/Navigability "Navigability") waterways on 7 rivers, mostly on the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube"), [Dnieper](/wiki/Dnieper "Dnieper") and [Pripyat](/wiki/Pripyat_(river) "Pripyat (river)"). All Ukraine's rivers freeze over in winter, limiting navigation.[[265]](#cite_note-271)

[Ukraine's rail network](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Ukraine "Rail transport in Ukraine") connects all major urban areas, port facilities and [industrial centres](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing"). The heaviest concentration of [railway track](/wiki/Railway_track "Railway track") is the [Donbas](/wiki/Donbas "Donbas") region.[[266]](#cite_note-272) Although [rail freight transport](/wiki/Rail_freight_transport "Rail freight transport") fell in the 1990s, Ukraine is still one of the [world's highest rail users](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_rail_usage "List of countries by rail usage").[[267]](#cite_note-273) Ukraine also has multiple [urban rail systems](/wiki/Urban_rail_transit "Urban rail transit"), particularly three metro ([Dnipro](/wiki/Dnipro_Metro "Dnipro Metro"), [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv_Metro "Kharkiv Metro"), and [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv_Metro "Kyiv Metro")),[[268]](#cite_note-274) two [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") ([Kryvyi Rih](/wiki/Kryvyi_Rih_Metrotram "Kryvyi Rih Metrotram") and [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv_Light_Rail "Kyiv Light Rail")),[[269]](#cite_note-275)[[270]](#cite_note-276) two urban *[elektrychka](/wiki/Elektrychka "Elektrychka")* (Kamianske–Dnipro–Synelnykove and [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv_Urban_Electric_Train "Kyiv Urban Electric Train")),[[271]](#cite_note-277) two [cable railway](/wiki/Cable_railway "Cable railway") ([Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv_Funicular "Kyiv Funicular") and [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa_Funicular "Odesa Funicular")),[[272]](#cite_note-278) and numerous [tramway](/wiki/List_of_town_tramway_systems_in_Ukraine "List of town tramway systems in Ukraine") systems.[[273]](#cite_note-279)

[Ukraine International Airlines](/wiki/Ukraine_International_Airlines "Ukraine International Airlines"), is the [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") and the largest [airline](/wiki/Airline "Airline"), with its head office in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv")[[274]](#cite_note-280) and its main hub at Kyiv's [Boryspil International Airport](/wiki/Boryspil_International_Airport "Boryspil International Airport"). It operated domestic and international passenger flights and cargo services to Europe, the Middle East, the United States,[[229]](#cite_note-auto1-235) Canada,[[275]](#cite_note-281) and Asia.

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Ukraine](/wiki/Energy_in_Ukraine "Energy in Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Electricity_production_by_source%2C_Ukraine%2C_OWID.svg/330px-Electricity_production_by_source%2C_Ukraine%2C_OWID.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Electricity_production_by_source,_Ukraine,_OWID.svg)

Electricity production by source in Ukraine

Energy in Ukraine is mainly from [gas](/wiki/Natural_gas_in_Ukraine "Natural gas in Ukraine") and [coal](/wiki/Coal_in_Ukraine "Coal in Ukraine"), followed by [nuclear](/wiki/Nuclear_power_in_Ukraine "Nuclear power in Ukraine") then [oil](/wiki/Oil_in_Ukraine "Oil in Ukraine").[[172]](#cite_note-:0-178) The coal industry has been disrupted by conflict.[[276]](#cite_note-282) Most gas and oil is imported, but since 2015 [energy policy](/wiki/Energy_policy "Energy policy") has prioritised diversifying energy supply.[[277]](#cite_note-283)

About half of [electricity generation](/wiki/Electricity_generation "Electricity generation") is nuclear and a quarter coal.[[172]](#cite_note-:0-178) The largest [nuclear power plant](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plant "Nuclear power plant") in Europe, the [Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Zaporizhzhia_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant"), is in Ukraine. [Fossil fuel subsidies](/wiki/Fossil_fuel_subsidies "Fossil fuel subsidies") were US$2.2 billion in 2019.[[278]](#cite_note-284) Until the 2010s all of Ukraine's nuclear fuel came from Russia, but now most does not.[[279]](#cite_note-285)

Some energy infrastructure was destroyed in the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)").[[280]](#cite_note-286)[[281]](#cite_note-287) The contract to transit [Russian gas](/wiki/Natural_gas_in_Russia "Natural gas in Russia") expires at the end of 2024.[[282]](#cite_note-288)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

In early 2022 Ukraine and [Moldova](/wiki/Energy_in_Moldova "Energy in Moldova") decoupled their electricity grids from the [Integrated Power System](/wiki/IPS/UPS#IPS "IPS/UPS") of Russia and [Belarus](/wiki/Energy_in_Belarus "Energy in Belarus"); and the [European Network of Transmission System Operators for Electricity](/wiki/European_Network_of_Transmission_System_Operators_for_Electricity "European Network of Transmission System Operators for Electricity") synchronised them with [continental Europe](/wiki/Synchronous_grid_of_Continental_Europe "Synchronous grid of Continental Europe").[[283]](#cite_note-cbsnews-ukraine-grid-289)[[284]](#cite_note-290)

### Information technology

Main articles: [Economy of Ukraine § Information technology](/wiki/Economy_of_Ukraine#Information_technology "Economy of Ukraine"), and [Internet in Ukraine](/wiki/Internet_in_Ukraine "Internet in Ukraine")

Key officials may use [Starlink](/wiki/Starlink "Starlink") as backup.[[285]](#cite_note-:3-291) The IT industry contributed almost 5 per cent to Ukraine's GDP in 2021[[286]](#cite_note-292) and in 2022 continued both inside and outside the country.[[287]](#cite_note-293)

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Ukraine](/wiki/Demographics_of_Ukraine "Demographics of Ukraine") and [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians")

Before the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), the country had an estimated population of over 41 million people, and was the [eighth-most populous country](/wiki/List_of_European_countries_by_population "List of European countries by population") in Europe. It is a [heavily urbanised country](/wiki/Urbanization_by_country "Urbanization by country"), and its industrial regions in the east and southeast are the most densely populated—about 67% of its total population lives in urban areas.[[288]](#cite_note-294) At that time Ukraine had a [population density](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population_density "List of countries by population density") of 69.5 inhabitants per square kilometre (180 inhabitants/mi2), and the overall [life expectancy in the country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_life_expectancy "List of countries by life expectancy") at birth was 73 years (68 years for males and 77.8 years for females).[[289]](#cite_note-295)

Following the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"), Ukraine's population hit a peak of roughly 52 million in 1993. However, due to its [death rate](/wiki/Death_rate "Death rate") exceeding its [birth rate](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate"), mass emigration, poor living conditions, and low-quality health care,[[290]](#cite_note-nw-20260217-296)[[291]](#cite_note-ukrstat-population-297) the total population decreased by 6.6 million, or 12.8% from the same year to 2014.

According to the [2001 census](/wiki/Ukrainian_Census_(2001) "Ukrainian Census (2001)"), ethnic [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") made up roughly 78% of the population, while [Russians](/wiki/Russians_in_Ukraine "Russians in Ukraine") were the largest minority, at some 17.3% of the population. Small minority populations included: [Belarusians](/wiki/Belarusians "Belarusians") (0.6%), [Moldovans](/wiki/Moldovans "Moldovans") (0.5%), [Crimean Tatars](/wiki/Crimean_Tatars "Crimean Tatars") (0.5%), [Bulgarians](/wiki/Bulgarians "Bulgarians") (0.4%), [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians") (0.3%), [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") (0.3%), [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") (0.3%), [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") (0.3%), [Armenians](/wiki/Armenians "Armenians") (0.2%), [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") (0.2%) and [Tatars](/wiki/Tatars "Tatars") (0.2%).[[2]](#cite_note-Ethnic_composition_of_the_population_of_Ukraine,_2001_Census-2) It was also estimated that there were about 10–40,000 [Koreans](/wiki/Koreans "Koreans") in Ukraine, who lived mostly in the south of the country, belonging to the historical [Koryo-saram](/wiki/Koryo-saram "Koryo-saram") group,[[292]](#cite_note-298)[[293]](#cite_note-299) as well as about 47,600 [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") (though the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") estimates a higher number of about 260,000).[[294]](#cite_note-300)

Outside the former Soviet Union, the largest source of incoming immigrants in Ukraine's post-independence period was from four Asian countries, namely China, India, Pakistan and Afghanistan.[[295]](#cite_note-mp-301) In the late 2010s 1.4 million Ukrainians were [internally displaced](/wiki/Internally_displaced "Internally displaced") due to the [war in Donbas](/wiki/War_in_Donbas_(2014%E2%80%932022) "War in Donbas (2014–2022)"),[[296]](#cite_note-302) and in early 2022, over 4.1 million fled the country after the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), causing the [Ukrainian refugee crisis](/wiki/Ukrainian_refugee_crisis "Ukrainian refugee crisis").[[297]](#cite_note-303) Most male Ukrainian nationals aged 18 to 60 were denied exit from Ukraine.[[298]](#cite_note-304) The Ukrainian government estimates that the population in the regions controlled by Ukraine was 25 to 27 million in 2024.[[299]](#cite_note-305)

Main article: [List of cities in Ukraine](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Ukraine "List of cities in Ukraine")

### Language

Main article: [Languages of Ukraine](/wiki/Languages_of_Ukraine "Languages of Ukraine")

Further information: [Ukrainian language](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language") and [Russian language in Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_language_in_Ukraine "Russian language in Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%BD%D1%961959%E2%80%942001.gif/330px-%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%BD%D1%961959%E2%80%942001.gif)](/wiki/File:%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%BD%D1%961959%E2%80%942001.gif)

During the post-WW2 period, the Russification of ethnic Ukrainians,[[302]](#cite_note-308) as well as the mass immigration of ethnic Russians,[[303]](#cite_note-309) significantly changed the ethnic and linguistic composition of Ukraine's population. The change was especially evident in Donbas, where Ukrainian speakers became a minority.

According to Ukraine's constitution, the [state language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") is [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language").[[304]](#cite_note-SerhyYUBoaMN-310) [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") is widely spoken in the country, especially in eastern and southern Ukraine.[[304]](#cite_note-SerhyYUBoaMN-310)[[305]](#cite_note-311) Most native Ukrainian speakers know Russian as a second language.[[304]](#cite_note-SerhyYUBoaMN-310) Russian was the *de facto* dominant language of the Soviet Union but Ukrainian also held official status in the republic,[[306]](#cite_note-312) and in the schools of the [Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Ukrainian_SSR "Ukrainian SSR"), learning Ukrainian was mandatory.[[304]](#cite_note-SerhyYUBoaMN-310)

Ukrainian is the primary language used in the vast majority of Ukraine. 67% of Ukrainians speak Ukrainian as their primary language, while 30% speak Russian as their primary language.[[307]](#cite_note-313) In eastern and southern Ukraine, Russian is the primary language in some cities, while Ukrainian is used in rural areas. [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language") is spoken in [Chernivtsi Oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast") and parts of [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa_Oblast "Odesa Oblast") and [Zakarpattia](/wiki/Zakarpattia_Oblast "Zakarpattia Oblast") oblasts,[[308]](#cite_note-314) while [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") is spoken in [Zakarpattia Oblast](/wiki/Zakarpattia_Oblast "Zakarpattia Oblast").[[309]](#cite_note-unian.info-315) There is no consensus among scholars whether [Rusyn](/wiki/Rusyn_language "Rusyn language"), also spoken in Zakarpattia, is a distinct language or a dialect of Ukrainian.[[310]](#cite_note-316) The Ukrainian government does not recognise Rusyn and [Rusyns](/wiki/Rusyns "Rusyns") as a distinct language and people.[[311]](#cite_note-317)

For a large part of the Soviet era, the number of Ukrainian speakers declined from generation to generation, and by the mid-1980s, the usage of the Ukrainian language in public life had decreased significantly.[[312]](#cite_note-Shamshur-318) Following independence, the government of Ukraine began restoring the use of the Ukrainian language in schools and government through a policy of [Ukrainisation](/wiki/Ukrainisation "Ukrainisation").[[313]](#cite_note-319)[[314]](#cite_note-320)

Effective in August 2012, [a new law on regional languages](/wiki/Legislation_on_languages_in_Ukraine "Legislation on languages in Ukraine") entitled any local language spoken by at least a 10 percent minority be declared official within that area.[[315]](#cite_note-321) Within weeks, Russian was declared a regional language of several southern and eastern [oblasts](/wiki/Oblasts_of_Ukraine "Oblasts of Ukraine") (provinces) and cities.[[316]](#cite_note-322) Russian could then be used in the administrative office work and documents of those places.[[317]](#cite_note-NewUklang2892012-323)[[318]](#cite_note-324)

In 2014, following the [Revolution of Dignity](/wiki/Revolution_of_Dignity "Revolution of Dignity"), the [Ukrainian Parliament](/wiki/Ukrainian_Parliament "Ukrainian Parliament") voted to repeal the [law on regional languages](/wiki/On_the_Principles_of_State_Language_Policy "On the Principles of State Language Policy"), making Ukrainian the sole state language at all levels; however, the repeal was not signed by acting [President Turchynov](/wiki/Oleksandr_Turchynov "Oleksandr Turchynov") or by President Poroshenko.[[319]](#cite_note-325)[[320]](#cite_note-326)[[321]](#cite_note-327) In 2019, the law allowing for official use of regional languages was found unconstitutional.[[322]](#cite_note-328) According to the Council of Europe, this act fails to achieve fair protection of the [linguistic rights](/wiki/Linguistic_rights "Linguistic rights") of [minorities](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language").[[323]](#cite_note-329) Presently, most foreign films and TV programmes, including Russian ones, are subtitled or dubbed in Ukrainian.[[324]](#cite_note-330) Ukraine's 2017 [education law](/wiki/Education_in_Ukraine "Education in Ukraine") bars primary education in public schools in grade five and up in any language but Ukrainian.[[325]](#cite_note-331)[[326]](#cite_note-332)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/UkraineNativeLanguagesCensus2001detailed-en.png/330px-UkraineNativeLanguagesCensus2001detailed-en.png)](/wiki/File:UkraineNativeLanguagesCensus2001detailed-en.png)

Linguistic map of Ukraine showing most common native language by city, town, or village council, according to the [2001 census](/wiki/Ukrainian_Census_(2001) "Ukrainian Census (2001)")

Since 2014, during the Russian-Ukrainian war, Russia has been pursuing a policy of forced Russification of [the territories of Ukraine it has occupied](/wiki/Russian-occupied_territories_of_Ukraine "Russian-occupied territories of Ukraine") — schools teach only in Russian, even in entirely Ukrainian-speaking settlements. Ukrainian school textbooks have been banned, and those who wish to study in Ukrainian are forced to do so in secret from the occupation authorities.[[327]](#cite_note-333)[[328]](#cite_note-334) Ukrainian-language literature is also being destroyed on a massive scale.[[329]](#cite_note-335) According to the report "Ukraine: Forced Russified Education Under Occupation" by [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch"), the Russian occupation authorities and other high-ranking Russian officials have taken and continue to take measures to eradicate the Ukrainian language in the occupied territories.[[330]](#cite_note-336) [Ukrainian children forcibly deported to Russia](/wiki/Child_abductions_in_the_Russo-Ukrainian_war "Child abductions in the Russo-Ukrainian war") are also subjected to Russification.[[331]](#cite_note-337)

At the same time, Russian armed aggression provoked significant changes in the Ukrainian society's perception of the language issue: a significant part of the formerly Russian-speaking population switched to speaking Ukrainian,[[332]](#cite_note-338)[[333]](#cite_note-339) and the popularity of the idea of granting Russian official status throughout all of Ukraine or certain regions of the country has reached its lowest level since polling began in 1997.[[334]](#cite_note-340)

### Diaspora

Main article: [Ukrainian diaspora](/wiki/Ukrainian_diaspora "Ukrainian diaspora")

The Ukrainian [diaspora](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora") comprises [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") and their descendants who live outside Ukraine around the world, especially those who maintain some kind of connection to the land of their ancestors and maintain their feeling of Ukrainian national identity within their own local community.[[335]](#cite_note-341) The Ukrainian diaspora is found throughout numerous regions worldwide including other [post-Soviet states](/wiki/Post-Soviet_states "Post-Soviet states") as well as in [Canada](/wiki/Canadian_Ukrainian "Canadian Ukrainian"),[[336]](#cite_note-Cecco_2022_u131-342) and other countries such as [Poland](/wiki/Ukrainians_in_Poland "Ukrainians in Poland"),[[337]](#cite_note-343) [the United States](/wiki/Ukrainian_Americans "Ukrainian Americans"),[[338]](#cite_note-344) the UK[[339]](#cite_note-345)[[340]](#cite_note-346) and [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil").[[341]](#cite_note-347)

The [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)") has led to the [Ukrainian refugee crisis](/wiki/Ukrainian_refugee_crisis "Ukrainian refugee crisis") in which millions of Ukrainian civilians moved to neighbouring countries. Most crossed into Poland, Slovakia, and the Czech Republic, and others proceeded to at least temporarily settle in Hungary, Moldova, Germany, Austria, Romania and other European countries.[[342]](#cite_note-348)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Ukraine](/wiki/Religion_in_Ukraine "Religion in Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/80-391-0151_Kyiv_St.Sophia%27s_Cathedral_RB_18_2_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-80-391-0151_Kyiv_St.Sophia%27s_Cathedral_RB_18_2_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:80-391-0151_Kyiv_St.Sophia%27s_Cathedral_RB_18_2_(cropped).jpg)

The [Saint Sophia Cathedral in Kyiv](/wiki/Saint_Sophia%27s_Cathedral,_Kyiv "Saint Sophia's Cathedral, Kyiv"), a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"),[[343]](#cite_note-349) is one of the main Christian cathedrals in Ukraine

Ukraine has the world's [second-largest Eastern Orthodox population](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy_by_country "Eastern Orthodoxy by country"), after Russia.[[344]](#cite_note-350)[[345]](#cite_note-351) A 2021 survey conducted by the [Kyiv International Institute of Sociology](/wiki/Kyiv_International_Institute_of_Sociology "Kyiv International Institute of Sociology") (KIIS) found that 82% of Ukrainians declared themselves to be religious, while 7% were [atheists](/wiki/Atheists "Atheists"), and a further 11% found it difficult to answer the question.[[346]](#cite_note-352) The level of religiosity in Ukraine was reported to be the highest in [Western Ukraine](/wiki/Western_Ukraine "Western Ukraine") (91%), and the lowest in the [Donbas](/wiki/Donbas "Donbas") (57%) and [Eastern Ukraine](/wiki/Eastern_Ukraine "Eastern Ukraine") (56%).[[347]](#cite_note-Razumkov2016Page27-353)

In 2019, 82% of Ukrainians were Christians; out of which 72.7% declared themselves to be [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), 8.8% [Ukrainian Greek Catholics](/wiki/Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church"), 2.3% [Protestants](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") and 0.9% [Latin Church Catholics](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church"). Other [Christians](/wiki/Christian "Christian") comprised 2.3%. [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") were the religions of 0.2% of the population each. According to the KIIS study, roughly 58.3% of the Ukrainian Orthodox population were members of the [Orthodox Church of Ukraine](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Orthodox Church of Ukraine"), and 25.4% were members of the [Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate)](/wiki/Ukrainian_Orthodox_Church_(Moscow_Patriarchate) "Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate)").[[348]](#cite_note-354) [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants_in_Ukraine "Protestants in Ukraine") are a growing community in Ukraine, who made up 1.9% of the population in 2016,[[349]](#cite_note-Razumkov2016Page29-355) but rose to 2.2% of the population in 2018.

### Health

Main article: [Health in Ukraine](/wiki/Health_in_Ukraine "Health in Ukraine")

See also: [Healthcare in Ukraine](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Ukraine "Healthcare in Ukraine")
