Central Municipal Hospital in Kyiv

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/%D0%A0%D0%BE%D0%B1%D0%BE%D1%87%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D1%97%D0%B7%D0%B4%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B7%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B0_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D0%B8_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%9E%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%89%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%83_08.jpg/250px-%D0%A0%D0%BE%D0%B1%D0%BE%D1%87%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D1%97%D0%B7%D0%B4%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B7%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B0_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D0%B8_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%9E%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%89%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%83_08.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A0%D0%BE%D0%B1%D0%BE%D1%87%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D1%97%D0%B7%D0%B4%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B7%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B0_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D0%B8_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%9E%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%89%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%83_08.jpg)

Ukrainian president Zelenskyy visiting a hospital in the [Odesa Oblast](/wiki/Odesa_Oblast "Odesa Oblast"), where injured Ukrainian defenders are seen treated in July 2022 during the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)")

Ukraine's healthcare system is state subsidised and freely available to all Ukrainian citizens and registered residents. However, it is not compulsory to be treated in a state-run hospital as a number of private medical complexes do exist nationwide.[[350]](#cite_note-356) The public sector employs most healthcare professionals, with those working for private medical centres typically also retaining their state employment as they are mandated to provide care at public health facilities on a regular basis.[[351]](#cite_note-357)

All of Ukraine's medical service providers and hospitals are subordinate to the [Ministry of Healthcare](/wiki/Ministry_of_Healthcare_(Ukraine) "Ministry of Healthcare (Ukraine)"), which provides oversight and scrutiny of general medical practice as well as being responsible for the day-to-day administration of the healthcare system. Despite this, standards of hygiene and patient-care have fallen.[[352]](#cite_note-358)

Ukraine faces a number of major public health issues[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and is considered to be in a demographic crisis because of its high death rate, low birth rate, and high emigration.[[353]](#cite_note-359) A factor contributing to the high death rate is a high [mortality rate](/wiki/Mortality_rate "Mortality rate") among working-age males from preventable causes such as [alcohol poisoning](/wiki/Alcohol_poisoning "Alcohol poisoning") and smoking.[[354]](#cite_note-worldbank1-360)

Active reformation of Ukraine's healthcare system was initiated right after the appointment of [Ulana Suprun](/wiki/Ulana_Suprun "Ulana Suprun") as a head of the [Ministry of Healthcare](/wiki/Ministry_of_Healthcare_(Ukraine) "Ministry of Healthcare (Ukraine)").[[355]](#cite_note-Rada_Reform-361) Assisted by deputy Pavlo Kovtoniuk, Suprun first changed the distribution of finances in healthcare.[[356]](#cite_note-Kovtoniuk-362) Funds must follow the patient. General practitioners will provide basic care for patients. The patient will have the right to choose one. Emergency medical service is considered to be fully funded by the state. [Emergency Medicine Reform](/wiki/Emergency_Medicine_Reform_in_Ukraine_since_2016 "Emergency Medicine Reform in Ukraine since 2016") is also an important part of the healthcare reform. In addition, patients who suffer from chronic diseases, which cause a high toll of disability and mortality, are provided with free or low-price medicine.[[357]](#cite_note-Drugs-363)

As a result of the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)"), millions in Ukraine suffered physical injuries and psychological traumas.[[358]](#cite_note-364) The [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") has documented over 2,254 attacks on healthcare in Ukraine since the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[359]](#cite_note-WHO-365) According to the October 2024 data of the World Health Organization Ukraine health needs assessment, 68% of Ukrainians reported that their health declined compared to the pre-war period.[[359]](#cite_note-WHO-365) The war with Russia worsened Ukrainian children physical and mental health.[[360]](#cite_note-366)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Ukraine](/wiki/Education_in_Ukraine "Education in Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Universidad_Roja_de_Kiev.jpg/250px-Universidad_Roja_de_Kiev.jpg)](/wiki/File:Universidad_Roja_de_Kiev.jpg)

The [University of Kyiv](/wiki/Taras_Shevchenko_National_University_of_Kyiv "Taras Shevchenko National University of Kyiv") is one of Ukraine's most important educational institutions.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/%D0%A0%D0%B5%D0%B7%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8F_%D0%BC%D0%B8%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%B8%D1%82%D1%96%D0%B2_%D0%91%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8_%D1%96_%D0%94%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%BC%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%97_5.jpg/250px-%D0%A0%D0%B5%D0%B7%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8F_%D0%BC%D0%B8%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%B8%D1%82%D1%96%D0%B2_%D0%91%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8_%D1%96_%D0%94%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%BC%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%97_5.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A0%D0%B5%D0%B7%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8F_%D0%BC%D0%B8%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%B8%D1%82%D1%96%D0%B2_%D0%91%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8_%D1%96_%D0%94%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%BC%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%97_5.jpg)

The [Residence of Bukovinian and Dalmatian Metropolitans](/wiki/Residence_of_Bukovinian_and_Dalmatian_Metropolitans "Residence of Bukovinian and Dalmatian Metropolitans") by [Josef Hlávka](/wiki/Josef_Hl%C3%A1vka "Josef Hlávka"), 1882, now [Chernivtsi University](/wiki/Chernivtsi_University "Chernivtsi University")

According to the [Ukrainian constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ukraine "Constitution of Ukraine"), access to free education is granted to all citizens. Complete general secondary education is compulsory in the state schools which constitute the overwhelming majority. Free higher education in state and communal educational establishments is provided on a competitive basis.[[361]](#cite_note-367)

Because of the Soviet Union's emphasis on total access of education for all citizens, which continues today, the [literacy rate](/wiki/Literacy_rate "Literacy rate") is an estimated 99.4%.[[43]](#cite_note-cia-47) Since 2005, an eleven-year school programme has been replaced with a twelve-year one: primary education takes four years to complete (starting at age six), middle education (secondary) takes five years to complete; upper secondary then takes three years.[[362]](#cite_note-368) Students in the 12th grade take Government tests, which are also referred to as school-leaving exams. These tests are later used for university admissions.

Among the oldest is also the [Lviv University](/wiki/Lviv_University "Lviv University"), founded in 1661. More higher education institutions were set up in the 19th century, beginning with universities in [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv_University "Kharkiv University") (1805), [Kyiv](/wiki/Taras_Shevchenko_National_University_of_Kyiv "Taras Shevchenko National University of Kyiv") (1834), [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa_University "Odesa University") (1865) and [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi_University "Chernivtsi University") (1875) and a number of professional higher education institutions, e.g.: [Nizhyn Historical and Philological Institute](/wiki/Nizhyn_Pedagogical_University "Nizhyn Pedagogical University") (originally established as the Gymnasium of Higher Sciences in 1805), a Veterinary Institute (1873) and a [Technological Institute](/wiki/Kharkiv_Polytechnical_Institute "Kharkiv Polytechnical Institute") (1885) in [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv"), a [Polytechnic Institute](/wiki/Igor_Sikorsky_Kyiv_Polytechnic_Institute "Igor Sikorsky Kyiv Polytechnic Institute") in Kyiv (1898) and a Higher Mining School (1899) in [Katerynoslav](/wiki/Dnipro "Dnipro"). Rapid growth followed in the [Soviet](/wiki/Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic") period. By 1988 the number of higher education institutions increased to 146 with over 850,000 students.[[363]](#cite_note-369)

The Ukrainian higher education system comprises higher educational establishments, [scientific](/wiki/Scientific "Scientific") and [methodological](/wiki/Methodological "Methodological") facilities under national, [municipal](/wiki/Municipal_government "Municipal government") and self-governing bodies in charge of education.[[364]](#cite_note-370) The organisation of higher education in Ukraine is built up in accordance with the structure of education of the world's higher [developed countries](/wiki/Developed_countries "Developed countries"), as is defined by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") and the UN.[[365]](#cite_note-371)

Ukraine produces the fourth largest number of [post-secondary graduates](/wiki/Tertiary_education "Tertiary education") in Europe, while being ranked seventh in population.[[366]](#cite_note-372) [Higher education](/wiki/Higher_education_in_Ukraine "Higher education in Ukraine") is either state funded or private. Most universities provide subsidised housing for out-of-city students. It is common for libraries to supply required books for all registered students. Ukrainian universities confer two degrees: the bachelor's degree (4 years) and the master's degree (5–6th year), in accordance with the [Bologna process](/wiki/Bologna_process "Bologna process"). Historically, [Specialist degree](/wiki/Specialist_degree "Specialist degree") (usually 5 years) is still also granted; it was the only degree awarded by universities in Soviet times.[[367]](#cite_note-373) Ukraine was ranked 66th in 2025 in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index").[[368]](#cite_note-374)[[369]](#cite_note-375)

### Regional differences

See also: [Demographics of Ukraine § Regional differences](/wiki/Demographics_of_Ukraine#Regional_differences "Demographics of Ukraine"), [Central Ukraine](/wiki/Central_Ukraine "Central Ukraine"), [Eastern Ukraine](/wiki/Eastern_Ukraine "Eastern Ukraine"), [Southern Ukraine](/wiki/Southern_Ukraine "Southern Ukraine"), and [Western Ukraine](/wiki/Western_Ukraine "Western Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Ukr_elections_2014_multimandate_okruhs.png/330px-Ukr_elections_2014_multimandate_okruhs.png)](/wiki/File:Ukr_elections_2014_multimandate_okruhs.png)

The results of the [2014 parliamentary election](/wiki/2014_Ukrainian_parliamentary_election "2014 Ukrainian parliamentary election") with [People's Front](/wiki/People%27s_Front_(Ukraine) "People's Front (Ukraine)") in yellow, [Opposition Bloc](/wiki/Opposition_Bloc "Opposition Bloc") in blue and [Petro Poroshenko Bloc](/wiki/Petro_Poroshenko_Bloc "Petro Poroshenko Bloc") in red

[Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language") is the dominant language in [Western Ukraine](/wiki/Western_Ukraine "Western Ukraine") and in [Central Ukraine](/wiki/Central_Ukraine "Central Ukraine"), while [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") is the dominant language in the cities of [Eastern Ukraine](/wiki/Eastern_Ukraine "Eastern Ukraine") and [Southern Ukraine](/wiki/Southern_Ukraine "Southern Ukraine"). In the [Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Ukrainian_SSR "Ukrainian SSR") schools, learning [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") was mandatory; in modern Ukraine, schools with Ukrainian as the language of instruction offer classes in Russian and in the other minority languages.[[304]](#cite_note-SerhyYUBoaMN-310)[[370]](#cite_note-376)[[371]](#cite_note-RatingJuly12-377)[[372]](#cite_note-378)

On the [Russian language](/wiki/Russian_language_in_Ukraine "Russian language in Ukraine"), on [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and [Ukrainian nationalism](/wiki/Ukrainian_nationalism "Ukrainian nationalism"), opinion in Eastern Ukraine and Southern Ukraine tends to be the exact opposite of those in Western Ukraine; while opinions in Central Ukraine on these topics tend be less extreme.[[371]](#cite_note-RatingJuly12-377)[[373]](#cite_note-379)[[374]](#cite_note-380)[[375]](#cite_note-KIISS1313-381)

Similar historical divisions also remain evident at the level of individual social identification. Attitudes toward the most important political issue, relations with Russia, differed strongly between [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv"), identifying more with [Ukrainian nationalism](/wiki/Ukrainian_nationalism "Ukrainian nationalism") and the [Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church"), and [Donetsk](/wiki/Donetsk "Donetsk"), predominantly Russian orientated and favourable to the [Soviet era](/wiki/Soviet_era "Soviet era"), while in central and southern Ukraine, as well as [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), such divisions were less important and there was less antipathy toward people from other regions.[[376]](#cite_note-antipathy-382)

However, all were united by an overarching Ukrainian identity based on shared economic difficulties, showing that other attitudes are determined more by culture and politics than by demographic differences.[[376]](#cite_note-antipathy-382)[[377]](#cite_note-383) Surveys of regional identities in Ukraine have shown that the feeling of belonging to a "Soviet identity" is strongest in the [Donbas](/wiki/Donbas "Donbas") (about 40%) and the Crimea (about 30%).[[378]](#cite_note-384)

During [elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Ukraine "Elections in Ukraine") voters of Western and Central Ukrainian [oblasts](/wiki/Oblasts_of_Ukraine "Oblasts of Ukraine") (provinces) vote mostly for parties ([Our Ukraine](/wiki/Our_Ukraine%E2%80%93People%27s_Self-Defense_Bloc "Our Ukraine–People's Self-Defense Bloc"), [Batkivshchyna](/wiki/All-Ukrainian_Union_%22Fatherland%22 "All-Ukrainian Union \"Fatherland\""))[[379]](#cite_note-385)[[380]](#cite_note-386) and presidential candidates ([Viktor Yuschenko](/wiki/Viktor_Yuschenko "Viktor Yuschenko"), [Yulia Tymoshenko](/wiki/Yulia_Tymoshenko "Yulia Tymoshenko")) with a [pro-Western](/wiki/Pro-Western "Pro-Western") and state reform [platform](/wiki/Political_platform "Political platform"), while voters in Southern and Eastern oblasts vote for parties ([CPU](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Ukraine "Communist Party of Ukraine"), [Party of Regions](/wiki/Party_of_Regions "Party of Regions")) and presidential candidates ([Viktor Yanukovych](/wiki/Viktor_Yanukovych "Viktor Yanukovych")) with a [pro-Russian](/wiki/Pro-Russian "Pro-Russian") and [status quo](/wiki/Status_quo "Status quo") platform.[[381]](#cite_note-EWparties-387)[[382]](#cite_note-Umland-388)[[383]](#cite_note-389)[[384]](#cite_note-390) However, this geographical division is decreasing.[[385]](#cite_note-391)[[386]](#cite_note-392)[[387]](#cite_note-393)

## Culture

Main article: [Ukrainian culture](/wiki/Ukrainian_culture "Ukrainian culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Pysanky2011.JPG/250px-Pysanky2011.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pysanky2011.JPG)

A collection of traditional Ukrainian Easter eggs—[pysanky](/wiki/Egg_decorating_in_Slavic_culture#Pysanky "Egg decorating in Slavic culture"). The design motifs on pysanky date back to early Slavic cultures

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Christmas_Vertep_in_Lviv._Photo_256.jpg/250px-Christmas_Vertep_in_Lviv._Photo_256.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christmas_Vertep_in_Lviv._Photo_256.jpg)

[Christmas](/wiki/Christmas_in_Ukraine "Christmas in Ukraine") celebration in [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv")

Ukrainian customs are heavily influenced by [Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), the dominant religion in the country.[[388]](#cite_note-394) Gender roles also tend to be more traditional, and grandparents play a greater role in bringing up children, than in the West.[[389]](#cite_note-395) The culture of Ukraine has also been influenced by its eastern and western neighbours, reflected in its [architecture](/wiki/Ukrainian_architecture "Ukrainian architecture"), music and art.[[390]](#cite_note-396)

The Communist era had quite a strong effect on the art and writing of Ukraine.[[391]](#cite_note-397) In 1932, Stalin made [socialist realism](/wiki/Socialist_realism "Socialist realism") state policy in the Soviet Union when he promulgated the decree "On the Reconstruction of Literary and Art Organisations". This greatly stifled creativity. During the 1980s [glasnost](/wiki/Glasnost "Glasnost") (openness) was introduced and Soviet artists and writers again became free to express themselves as they wanted.[[392]](#cite_note-398)

As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ukraine&action=edit), UNESCO inscribed [8 properties](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Ukraine "List of World Heritage Sites in Ukraine") in Ukraine on the [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_List "World Heritage List"). Ukraine is also known for its decorative and folk traditions such as [Petrykivka painting](/wiki/Petrykivka_painting "Petrykivka painting"), [Kosiv ceramics](/wiki/Kosiv_painted_ceramics "Kosiv painted ceramics"), and [Cossack songs](/wiki/Cossack_songs "Cossack songs").[[393]](#cite_note-399)[[394]](#cite_note-400)[[395]](#cite_note-401)[[396]](#cite_note-402) Between February 2022 and March 2023, UNESCO verified the damage to 247 sites, including 107 [religious sites](/wiki/Religious_site "Religious site"), 89 buildings of artistic or historical interest, 19 monuments and 12 libraries.[[397]](#cite_note-403) Since January 2023, the [historic centre](/wiki/Historic_Centre_of_Odesa "Historic Centre of Odesa") of [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa "Odesa") has been inscribed on the [List of World Heritage in Danger](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_in_Danger "List of World Heritage in Danger").[[398]](#cite_note-404)

The tradition of the [Easter eggs](/wiki/Easter_egg "Easter egg"), known as [pysanky](/wiki/Egg_decorating_in_Slavic_culture#Pysanky "Egg decorating in Slavic culture"), has long roots in Ukraine. These eggs were drawn on with wax to create a pattern; then, the dye was applied to give the eggs their pleasant colours, the dye did not affect the previously wax-coated parts of the egg. After the entire egg was dyed, the wax was removed leaving only the colourful pattern. This tradition is thousands of years old, and precedes the arrival of Christianity to Ukraine.[[399]](#cite_note-405) In the city of [Kolomyia](/wiki/Kolomyia "Kolomyia") near the foothills of the [Carpathian Mountains](/wiki/Carpathian_Mountains "Carpathian Mountains"), the [museum of Pysanka](/wiki/Pysanka_Museum "Pysanka Museum") was built in 2000 and won a nomination as the monument of modern Ukraine in 2007, part of the [Seven Wonders of Ukraine](/wiki/Seven_Wonders_of_Ukraine "Seven Wonders of Ukraine") action.

Since 2012, the Ministry of Culture of Ukraine has formed the [National Register of Elements of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Ukraine](/wiki/List_of_Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_elements_in_Ukraine#National_Register "List of Intangible Cultural Heritage elements in Ukraine"),[[400]](#cite_note-406) which consists of 115 items as of September 2025.[[401]](#cite_note-National_Register-407)

### Architecture

Main article: [Ukrainian architecture](/wiki/Ukrainian_architecture "Ukrainian architecture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/80-391-9007_Kyiv_St.Michael%27s_Golden-Domed_Monastery_RB_18.jpg/250px-80-391-9007_Kyiv_St.Michael%27s_Golden-Domed_Monastery_RB_18.jpg)](/wiki/File:80-391-9007_Kyiv_St.Michael%27s_Golden-Domed_Monastery_RB_18.jpg)

[St Michael's Golden-Domed Cathedral](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Golden-Domed_Monastery "St. Michael's Golden-Domed Monastery") in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), the foremost example[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Ukraine#Dubious "Talk:Ukraine")*] of [Ukrainian Baroque](/wiki/Ukrainian_Baroque "Ukrainian Baroque") and one of Ukraine's most recognisable landmarks

Ukrainian architecture includes the motifs and styles that are found in structures built in modern Ukraine, and by [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") worldwide. These include [initial roots](/wiki/Architecture_of_Kievan_Rus%27 "Architecture of Kievan Rus'") which were established in the state of [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'"). Following the [Christianisation of Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Christianization_of_Kievan_Rus%27 "Christianization of Kievan Rus'"), Ukrainian architecture has been influenced by [Byzantine architecture](/wiki/Byzantine_architecture "Byzantine architecture"). After the [Mongol invasion of Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Kievan_Rus%27 "Mongol invasion of Kievan Rus'"), the [Galician style](/wiki/Galician_school_(architecture) "Galician school (architecture)") continued to develop in the [Kingdom of Galicia–Volhynia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia%E2%80%93Volhynia "Kingdom of Galicia–Volhynia").[[402]](#cite_note-KatchanovskiKohutNebesio2013-408)

After the union with the [Tsardom of Russia](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Russia "Tsardom of Russia"), architecture in Ukraine began to develop in different directions, with many structures in the larger eastern, Russian-ruled area built in the styles of [Russian architecture](/wiki/Russian_architecture "Russian architecture") of that period, whilst the western region of [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Central_Europe) "Galicia (Central Europe)") developed under [Polish](/wiki/Architecture_of_Poland "Architecture of Poland") and [Austro-Hungarian architectural influences](/wiki/Architecture_of_Austria "Architecture of Austria").[[402]](#cite_note-KatchanovskiKohutNebesio2013-408) Still, a separate [Ukrainian Baroque style](/wiki/Ukrainian_Baroque "Ukrainian Baroque") was developed by the [Ukrainian Cossacks](/wiki/Ukrainian_Cossacks "Ukrainian Cossacks") in 17th–18th centuries,[[403]](#cite_note-:42-409) and [Ukrainian Art Nouveau](/wiki/Ukrainian_Art_Nouveau "Ukrainian Art Nouveau") had limited success in the 20th century.[[404]](#cite_note-410) Ukrainian national motifs would eventually be used during the period of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and in modern independent Ukraine.[[402]](#cite_note-KatchanovskiKohutNebesio2013-408) However, much of the contemporary architectural skyline of Ukraine is dominated by Soviet-style [Khrushchyovkas](/wiki/Khrushchyovka "Khrushchyovka"), or low-cost apartment buildings.[[405]](#cite_note-411)

### Libraries

See also: [List of libraries in Ukraine](/wiki/List_of_libraries_in_Ukraine "List of libraries in Ukraine")

The [Vernadsky National Library of Ukraine](/wiki/Vernadsky_National_Library_of_Ukraine "Vernadsky National Library of Ukraine"), is the main academic library and main scientific information centre in Ukraine.

During the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)"), the Russians bombed the Maksymovych Scientific Library of the Taras Shevchenko Kyiv National University, [Vernadsky National Library of Ukraine](/wiki/Vernadsky_National_Library_of_Ukraine "Vernadsky National Library of Ukraine"), the National Scientific Medical Library of Ukraine and the Kyiv City Youth Library.[[406]](#cite_note-412)

### Literature

Main article: [Ukrainian literature](/wiki/Ukrainian_literature "Ukrainian literature")

Ukrainian literature has origins in [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic") writings, which was used as a [liturgical](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") and [literary language](/wiki/Literary_language "Literary language") following [Christianisation](/wiki/Christianization_of_Kievan_Rus%27 "Christianization of Kievan Rus'") in the 10th and 11th centuries.[[407]](#cite_note-ualit-413)[[408]](#cite_note-ualitmsn-414)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[g]](#cite_note-415) Other writings from the time include [chronicles](/wiki/Chronicle "Chronicle"), the most significant of which was the *[Primary Chronicle](/wiki/Primary_Chronicle "Primary Chronicle")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Literary activity faced a sudden decline after the [Mongol invasion of Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Kievan_Rus%27 "Mongol invasion of Kievan Rus'"), before seeing a revival beginning in the 14th century, and was advanced in the 16th century with the invention of the [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press").[[407]](#cite_note-ualit-413)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/%D0%A2._%D0%93._%D0%A8%D0%B5%D0%B2%D1%87%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE._%D0%9A%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%8C_1859.jpg/250px-%D0%A2._%D0%93._%D0%A8%D0%B5%D0%B2%D1%87%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE._%D0%9A%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%8C_1859.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A2._%D0%93._%D0%A8%D0%B5%D0%B2%D1%87%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE._%D0%9A%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%8C_1859.jpg)

[Taras Shevchenko](/wiki/Taras_Shevchenko "Taras Shevchenko")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Lesya_Ukrainka_portrait.jpg/250px-Lesya_Ukrainka_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lesya_Ukrainka_portrait.jpg)

[Lesya Ukrainka](/wiki/Lesya_Ukrainka "Lesya Ukrainka"), one of the foremost Ukrainian women writers

The [Cossacks](/wiki/Cossacks "Cossacks") established an independent society and popularised a [new kind](/wiki/Duma_(epic) "Duma (epic)") of [epic poem](/wiki/Epic_poetry "Epic poetry"), which marked a high point of Ukrainian [oral literature](/wiki/Oral_literature "Oral literature").[[408]](#cite_note-ualitmsn-414)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] These advances were then set back in the 17th and early 18th centuries, as many Ukrainian authors wrote in Russian or Polish. Nonetheless, by the late 18th century, the modern literary Ukrainian language finally emerged.[[407]](#cite_note-ualit-413) In 1798, the modern era of the Ukrainian literary tradition began with [Ivan Kotliarevsky](/wiki/Ivan_Kotliarevsky "Ivan Kotliarevsky")'s publication of [Eneida](/wiki/Eneida "Eneida") in the Ukrainian vernacular.[[409]](#cite_note-416)

By the 1830s, a Ukrainian [romantic literature](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") began to develop, and the nation's most renowned cultural figure, romanticist poet-painter [Taras Shevchenko](/wiki/Taras_Shevchenko "Taras Shevchenko") emerged. Whereas Ivan Kotliarevsky is considered to be the father of literature in the Ukrainian vernacular; Shevchenko is the father of a national revival.[[410]](#cite_note-417)

Then, in 1863, the use of the Ukrainian language in print was effectively [prohibited](/wiki/Ems_Ukaz "Ems Ukaz") by the Russian Empire.[[66]](#cite_note-censor-70) This severely curtailed literary activity in the area, and Ukrainian writers were forced to either publish their works in Russian or release them in Austrian controlled [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Central_Europe) "Galicia (Central Europe)"). The ban was never officially lifted, but it became obsolete after the revolution and the Bolsheviks' coming to power.[[408]](#cite_note-ualitmsn-414)

Ukrainian literature continued to flourish in the early Soviet years when nearly all literary trends were approved. These policies faced a steep decline in the 1930s, when prominent representatives as well as many others were killed by the [NKVD](/wiki/NKVD "NKVD") during the [Great Purge](/wiki/Great_Purge "Great Purge"). In general around 223 writers were repressed by what was known as the [Executed Renaissance](/wiki/Executed_Renaissance "Executed Renaissance").[[411]](#cite_note-418) These repressions were part of Stalin's implemented policy of [socialist realism](/wiki/Socialist_realism "Socialist realism"). The doctrine did not necessarily repress the use of the Ukrainian language, but it required that writers follow a certain style in their works.

Literary freedom grew in the late 1980s and early 1990s alongside the decline and collapse of the USSR and the reestablishment of Ukrainian independence in 1991.[[407]](#cite_note-ualit-413)

### Weaving and embroidery

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Rushnyk_Ukraine_embroidered_decorative_towels.jpg/250px-Rushnyk_Ukraine_embroidered_decorative_towels.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rushnyk_Ukraine_embroidered_decorative_towels.jpg)

[Rushnyk](/wiki/Rushnyk "Rushnyk"), [Ukrainian embroidery](/wiki/Ukrainian_embroidery "Ukrainian embroidery")

Artisan [textile arts](/wiki/Textile_arts "Textile arts") play an important role in Ukrainian culture,[[412]](#cite_note-419) especially in [Ukrainian wedding traditions](/wiki/Ukrainian_wedding_traditions "Ukrainian wedding traditions"). [Ukrainian embroidery](/wiki/Ukrainian_embroidery "Ukrainian embroidery"), [weaving](/wiki/Weaving "Weaving") and lace-making are used in traditional [folk dress](/wiki/Folk_dress "Folk dress") and in traditional celebrations. Ukrainian embroidery varies depending on the region of origin[[413]](#cite_note-420) and the designs have a long history of motifs, compositions, choice of colours and types of stitches.[[414]](#cite_note-museum-421) Use of colour is very important and has roots in [Ukrainian folklore](/wiki/Ukrainian_folklore "Ukrainian folklore"). Embroidery motifs found in different parts of Ukraine are preserved in the [Rushnyk](/wiki/Rushnyk "Rushnyk") Museum in [Pereiaslav](/wiki/Pereiaslav "Pereiaslav").

National dress is woven and highly decorated. Weaving with handmade looms is still practised in the village of Krupove, situated in [Rivne Oblast](/wiki/Rivne_Oblast "Rivne Oblast"). The village is the birthplace of two internationally recognised personalities in the scene of national crafts fabrication: Nina Myhailivna[[415]](#cite_note-422) and Uliana Petrivna.[[416]](#cite_note-423)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Ukraine](/wiki/Music_of_Ukraine "Music of Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Fedir_Stovbynenko_-_Kozak-bandyryst_%281890%29.jpg/250px-Fedir_Stovbynenko_-_Kozak-bandyryst_%281890%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fedir_Stovbynenko_-_Kozak-bandyryst_(1890).jpg)

[Cossack Mamay](/wiki/Cossack_Mamay "Cossack Mamay") playing a [kobza](/wiki/Kobza "Kobza")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/%D0%9B%D0%B8%D1%81%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE_%D0%9C%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%B0_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-%D0%9B%D0%B8%D1%81%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE_%D0%9C%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%B0_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9B%D0%B8%D1%81%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE_%D0%9C%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%B0_(cropped).jpg)

[Mykola Lysenko](/wiki/Mykola_Lysenko "Mykola Lysenko") is widely considered to be the father of Ukrainian classical music[[417]](#cite_note-Risch_2011_p._44-424)

Music is a major part of Ukrainian culture, with a long history and many influences. From traditional [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), to [classical](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") and [modern rock](/wiki/Modern_rock "Modern rock"), Ukraine has produced several internationally recognised musicians including [Kirill Karabits](/wiki/Kirill_Karabits "Kirill Karabits"), [Okean Elzy](/wiki/Okean_Elzy "Okean Elzy") and [Ruslana](/wiki/Ruslana "Ruslana"). Elements from traditional Ukrainian folk music made their way into Western music and even into modern [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"). Ukrainian music sometimes presents a perplexing mix of exotic melismatic singing with chordal harmony. The most striking general characteristic of authentic ethnic Ukrainian folk music is the wide use of minor modes or keys which incorporate augmented second intervals.[[418]](#cite_note-Sonevytsky_2019_p.-425)

During the Baroque period, music had a place of considerable importance in the curriculum of the [Kyiv-Mohyla Academy](/wiki/Kyiv-Mohyla_Academy "Kyiv-Mohyla Academy"). Much of the nobility was well versed in music with many Ukrainian Cossack leaders such as ([Mazepa](/wiki/Ivan_Mazepa "Ivan Mazepa"), [Paliy](/wiki/Semen_Paliy "Semen Paliy"), [Holovatyj](/wiki/Antin_Holovaty "Antin Holovaty"), [Sirko](/wiki/Ivan_Sirko "Ivan Sirko")) being accomplished players of the [kobza](/wiki/Kobza "Kobza"), [bandura](/wiki/Bandura "Bandura") or [torban](/wiki/Torban "Torban").

The first dedicated musical academy was set up in [Hlukhiv](/wiki/Hlukhiv "Hlukhiv") in 1738 and students were taught to sing and play violin and bandura from manuscripts. As a result, many of the earliest composers and performers within the Russian empire were ethnically Ukrainian, having been born or educated in Hlukhiv or having been closely associated with this music school.[[419]](#cite_note-Struk_1993_p._1461-426) Ukrainian classical music differs considerably depending on whether the composer was of Ukrainian ethnicity living in Ukraine, a composer of non-Ukrainian ethnicity who was a citizen of Ukraine, or part of the [Ukrainian diaspora](/wiki/Ukrainian_diaspora "Ukrainian diaspora").[[420]](#cite_note-Ukrainian_people_2017-427)

Since the mid-1960s, Western-influenced pop music has been growing in popularity in Ukraine. Folk singer and harmonium player [Mariana Sadovska](/wiki/Mariana_Sadovska "Mariana Sadovska") is prominent. Ukrainian pop and folk music arose with the international popularity of groups and performers like [Vopli Vidoplyasova](/wiki/Vopli_Vidoplyasova "Vopli Vidoplyasova"), [Dakh Daughters](/wiki/Dakh_Daughters "Dakh Daughters"), [Dakha Brakha](/wiki/Dakha_Brakha "Dakha Brakha"), [Ivan Dorn](/wiki/Ivan_Dorn "Ivan Dorn") and [Okean Elzy](/wiki/Okean_Elzy "Okean Elzy").

### Media

Main article: [Media of Ukraine](/wiki/Media_of_Ukraine "Media of Ukraine")

The Ukrainian legal framework on media freedom is deemed "among the most progressive in eastern Europe", although implementation has been uneven.[[421]](#cite_note-FH-428)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] The constitution and laws provide for [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech")[[422]](#cite_note-429) and [press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press_in_Ukraine "Freedom of the press in Ukraine"). The main regulatory authority for the broadcast media is the [National Television and Radio Broadcasting Council of Ukraine](/wiki/National_Television_and_Radio_Broadcasting_Council_of_Ukraine "National Television and Radio Broadcasting Council of Ukraine") (NTRBCU), tasked with licencing media outlets and ensure their compliance with the law.[[423]](#cite_note-430)

[Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv") dominates the media sector in Ukraine: National [newspapers](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_Ukraine "List of newspapers in Ukraine") *[Den](/wiki/Den_(newspaper) "Den (newspaper)")*, *[Dzerkalo Tyzhnia](/wiki/Dzerkalo_Tyzhnia "Dzerkalo Tyzhnia")*, tabloids, such as *[The Ukrainian Week](/wiki/The_Ukrainian_Week "The Ukrainian Week")* or *[Focus](/wiki/Focus_(Ukrainian_magazine) "Focus (Ukrainian magazine)")*, and television and radio are largely based there,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] although [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv") is also a significant national media centre. The National News Agency of Ukraine, [Ukrinform](/wiki/Ukrinform "Ukrinform") was founded here in 1918. [BBC Ukrainian](/wiki/BBC_Ukrainian "BBC Ukrainian") started its broadcasts in 1992.[[424]](#cite_note-431) As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ukraine&action=edit) 75% of the population use the internet, and social media is widely used by government and people.[[425]](#cite_note-432)

On 10 March 2024, creators of a documentary film *[20 Days in Mariupol](/wiki/20_Days_in_Mariupol "20 Days in Mariupol")* were awarded with the [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") in the category "Best Documentary Feature Film", the first Oscar in Ukraine's history.[[426]](#cite_note-433)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Ukrainian cuisine](/wiki/Ukrainian_cuisine "Ukrainian cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Borscht_served.jpg/250px-Borscht_served.jpg)](/wiki/File:Borscht_served.jpg)

[Borscht](/wiki/Borscht "Borscht") with [smetana](/wiki/Smetana_(dairy_product) "Smetana (dairy product)") ([sour cream](/wiki/Sour_cream "Sour cream"))

Ukrainian cuisine has been formed by the nation's tumultuous history, geography, culture and social customs. [Chicken](/wiki/Chicken "Chicken") is the most consumed type of [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein"), accounting for about half of the meat intake. It is followed by [pork](/wiki/Pork "Pork") and [beef](/wiki/Beef "Beef").[[427]](#cite_note-434): 12 Vegetables such as [potatoes](/wiki/Potato "Potato"), [cabbages](/wiki/Cabbage "Cabbage"), [mushrooms](/wiki/Mushroom "Mushroom") and [beetroots](/wiki/Beetroot "Beetroot") are widely consumed.[[428]](#cite_note-435) [Pickled vegetables](/wiki/Pickling "Pickling") are considered a delicacy.[[429]](#cite_note-436)[[430]](#cite_note-437) [Salo](/wiki/Salo_(food) "Salo (food)"), which is [cured](/wiki/Curing_(food_preservation) "Curing (food preservation)") pork fat, is considered the national delicacy.[[431]](#cite_note-438) Widely used [herbs](/wiki/Herb "Herb") include [dill](/wiki/Dill "Dill"), [parsley](/wiki/Parsley "Parsley"), [basil](/wiki/Basil "Basil"), [coriander](/wiki/Coriander "Coriander") and [chives](/wiki/Chives "Chives").[[432]](#cite_note-hercules-439)

Ukraine is often called the "[Breadbasket of Europe](/wiki/Breadbasket#Europe "Breadbasket")", and its plentiful [grain](/wiki/Grain "Grain") and [cereal](/wiki/Cereal "Cereal") resources such as [rye](/wiki/Rye "Rye") and [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat") play an important part in its cuisine; essential in making various kinds of bread.[[433]](#cite_note-bbccuisine-440)[[434]](#cite_note-441) [Chernozem](/wiki/Chernozem "Chernozem"), the country's black-coloured highly fertile soil, produces some of the world's most flavourful crops.[[435]](#cite_note-442)

Popular traditional dishes **[varenyky](/wiki/Varenyky "Varenyky")** ([dumplings](/wiki/Pierogi#Ukraine "Pierogi")), *[nalysnyky](/wiki/Nalysnyky "Nalysnyky")* ([crêpes](/wiki/Cr%C3%AApe "Crêpe")), *[kapusnyak](/wiki/Cabbage_soup "Cabbage soup")* (cabbage [soup](/wiki/Soup "Soup")), [borscht](/wiki/Borscht "Borscht") ([sour soup](/wiki/List_of_sour_soups "List of sour soups")) and *[holubtsi](/wiki/Go%C5%82%C4%85bki "Gołąbki")* ([cabbage rolls](/wiki/Cabbage_roll "Cabbage roll")).[[433]](#cite_note-bbccuisine-440) Among traditional baked goods are decorated [korovai](/wiki/Korovai "Korovai") and [paska](/wiki/Paska_(bread) "Paska (bread)") ([easter bread](/wiki/Easter_bread "Easter bread")).[[436]](#cite_note-443) Ukrainian specialties also include [Chicken Kiev](/wiki/Chicken_Kiev "Chicken Kiev")[[432]](#cite_note-hercules-439) and [Kyiv cake](/wiki/Kyiv_cake "Kyiv cake"). Popular drinks include *uzvar* ([kompot](/wiki/Kompot "Kompot") made of dried fruits),[[432]](#cite_note-hercules-439)[[437]](#cite_note-hill-444) *[ryazhanka](/wiki/Ryazhenka "Ryazhenka")*,[[438]](#cite_note-445) and **[horilka](/wiki/Horilka "Horilka")**.[[432]](#cite_note-hercules-439)[[437]](#cite_note-hill-444) [Liquor](/wiki/Liquor "Liquor") (spirits) are the most consumed type of [alcoholic beverage](/wiki/Alcoholic_beverage "Alcoholic beverage").[[439]](#cite_note-pmid-446) Alcohol consumption has seen a stark decrease, though by per capita, it remains [among the highest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_alcohol_consumption_per_capita "List of countries by alcohol consumption per capita").[[440]](#cite_note-447)[[439]](#cite_note-pmid-446)

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Ukraine](/wiki/Sport_in_Ukraine "Sport in Ukraine")

Ukraine greatly benefited from the Soviet emphasis on [physical education](/wiki/Physical_education "Physical education"). These policies left Ukraine with hundreds of stadia, swimming pools, gymnasia and many other athletic facilities.[[441]](#cite_note-448) The most popular sport is [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). The top professional league is the [Vyscha Liha](/wiki/Ukrainian_Premier_League "Ukrainian Premier League") ("premier league").

Many Ukrainians also played for the [Soviet national football team](/wiki/Soviet_national_football_team "Soviet national football team"), most notably [Ballon d'Or](/wiki/Ballon_d%27Or "Ballon d'Or") winners [Ihor Belanov](/wiki/Ihor_Belanov "Ihor Belanov") and [Oleh Blokhin](/wiki/Oleh_Blokhin "Oleh Blokhin"). This award was only presented to one Ukrainian after the dissolution of the Soviet Union, [Andriy Shevchenko](/wiki/Andriy_Shevchenko "Andriy Shevchenko"). The national team made its debut in the [2006 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup"), and reached the quarterfinals before losing to eventual champions, [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team").

Ukrainian [boxers](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") are amongst the best in the world.[[442]](#cite_note-449) Since becoming the undisputed cruiserweight champion in 2018, [Oleksandr Usyk](/wiki/Oleksandr_Usyk "Oleksandr Usyk") has also gone on to win the unified WBA (Super), IBF, WBO and IBO heavyweight titles. This feat made him one of only three boxers to have unified the cruiserweight world titles and become a world heavyweight champion.[[443]](#cite_note-450) The brothers [Vitali](/wiki/Vitali_Klitschko "Vitali Klitschko") and [Wladimir Klitschko](/wiki/Wladimir_Klitschko "Wladimir Klitschko") are former [heavyweight](/wiki/Heavyweight "Heavyweight") world champions who held multiple world titles throughout their careers. Also hailing from Ukraine is [Vasyl Lomachenko](/wiki/Vasyl_Lomachenko "Vasyl Lomachenko"), a [2008](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics") and [2012 Olympic](/wiki/2012_Olympic_games "2012 Olympic games") gold medalist. He is the [unified](/wiki/Unified_champion "Unified champion") [lightweight](/wiki/Lightweight "Lightweight") world champion who ties the record for winning a world title in the fewest professional fights; three.[[444]](#cite_note-451)

[Sergey Bubka](/wiki/Sergey_Bubka "Sergey Bubka") held the record in the [Pole vault](/wiki/Pole_vault "Pole vault") from 1993 to 2014; with great strength, speed and gymnastic abilities, he was voted the world's best athlete on several occasions.[[445]](#cite_note-452)[[446]](#cite_note-453)

[Basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") has gained popularity in Ukraine. In 2011, Ukraine was granted a right to organise [EuroBasket 2015](/wiki/EuroBasket_2015 "EuroBasket 2015"). Two years later the [Ukraine national basketball team](/wiki/Ukraine_national_basketball_team "Ukraine national basketball team") finished sixth in [EuroBasket 2013](/wiki/EuroBasket_2013 "EuroBasket 2013") and qualified to [FIBA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup "2014 FIBA Basketball World Cup") for the first time in its history. [Euroleague](/wiki/Euroleague "Euroleague") participant [Budivelnyk Kyiv](/wiki/BC_Budivelnyk "BC Budivelnyk") is the strongest professional basketball club in Ukraine.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess") is a popular sport in Ukraine. [Ruslan Ponomariov](/wiki/Ruslan_Ponomariov "Ruslan Ponomariov") is the former world champion. There are about 85 [Grandmasters](/wiki/Grandmaster_(chess) "Grandmaster (chess)") and 198 [International Masters](/wiki/International_Masters "International Masters") in Ukraine. [Rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") is played throughout Ukraine.[[447]](#cite_note-454)
